data.title: Silicon Valley’s Hollywood takeover is almost complete
data.description:
Asked about the impact of streaming on traditional cable networks, David Zaslav was blunt.

“Netflix never should’ve happened,” he said in an interview in 2015, lamenting how slow his industry had been to adapt to technological change.

A decade later those words are likely to resonate ever more with Zaslav, who now leads Warner Bros Discovery (WBD). Netflix, once the scrappy streaming challenger, has struck an $83bn (£62bn) deal to take control of WBD’s studio and streaming business.

Zaslav – who is one of Hollywood’s highest-paid executives with an annual pay package topping $50m – will probably emerge unscathed from the transaction.

More broadly, however, Netflix’s shock victory in a fiercely competitive bidding war signals a major reshaping of the US media landscape.

After more than a century under the auspices of California’s liberal media elite, Hollywood is falling ever further under the iron grip of the tech bros.

WBD’s appeal to Netflix is self-evident. The Hollywood studio boasts one of the strongest libraries of film and TV series – ranging from the Harry Potter franchise and DC Universe hits such as Batman to classics including Casablanca and Citizen Kane and perennial TV favourite Friends.

Through HBO, the streaming giant will also gain some of the biggest TV dramas in recent years – including Game of Thrones, Succession, The White Lotus and The Last of Us.

The value of this extraordinary back catalogue is evident in the price. Netflix is coughing up $27.75 per share for the assets, implying an enterprise value of $82.7bn.

Netflix’s successful bid, while not entirely out of the blue, has shocked many onlookers. Paramount, which was recently acquired for $8bn by the billionaire Ellison family, was widely considered the frontrunner.

Paramount, whose second largest shareholder is RedBird Capital, the US private equity fund that last month pulled out of its bid to buy The Telegraph, has been on a feverish acquisition spree since the summer.

Unlike other suitors, it was preparing a bid for the whole of WBD – including networks such as CNN.

The Ellison family’s ties to Donald Trump further boosted their credentials. The US president is believed to have supported a Paramount bid, which would have put his political ally in control of Left-leaning CNN.

Paramount’s fury at its impending defeat was evident in a letter sent to Zaslav last night, which accused WBD of having “abandoned the semblance and reality of a fair transaction process” and “embarked on a myopic process with a predetermined outcome that favours a single bidder”.

Netflix also saw off competition from Comcast, the US owner of Sky. The defeat will spark further soul-searching at the British broadcaster, which was already gearing up for the loss of its exclusive rights to HBO shows when the HBO Max service launches in the UK in March.

Sky has long presented itself as an “aggregator”, providing a one-stop shop for the plethora of different streaming services on offer. As this array of streaming services consolidates, the value of Sky’s position in the market will be eroded.

In a sign of its shifting priorities, the company is now in discussions to buy up ITV’s broadcasting business for £1.6bn.

Netflix’s clean sweep

The bidding war has been a blockbuster Hollywood spectacle for media watchers on both sides of the Atlantic. Beneath the drama and theatrics, however, a much bigger story is unfolding.

Netflix’s acquisition of WBD cements its astonishing rise from mail order-DVD provider to doyen of global entertainment.

When Netflix launched its streaming service back in 2007, it was viewed with relative indifference by Hollywood’s incumbent – who were happy to offer up their films and shows for some extra licencing revenues.

But as the Los Gatos-based company began making its own programming, it soon posed a direct challenge.

Thus began a period of profligate spending, as incumbents and new rivals battled it out to win over subscribers – whose demand for more entertainment reached unprecedented heights during Covid-induced lockdowns.

To the victor, the spoils. Netflix, having emerged triumphant from the streaming wars, is now cementing its dominance over Hollywood.

It is not just Netflix, however. In 2022, Amazon, which has muscled into the streaming market through its Prime Video service, splashed out $8.5bn to acquire MGM, the studio behind the James Bond franchise.

The same year, Apple staked its claim on Hollywood by winning the best picture Oscar for its feature film CODA. Paramount, meanwhile, is now controlled by Larry Ellison, the Oracle founder who has used his tech wealth to tighten his grip on media.

Disney and Comcast-owned Universal are now the two surviving major studios beyond Silicon Valley’s grasp.

Questions will now be raised about the future of Warner Bros Discovery’s traditional networks, which include CNN, TNT Sports in the US and Discovery.

The company will spin off these assets prior to the Netflix takeover – leaving them on the block for any interested bidder.

End of traditional cinema

And while WBD has hailed the deal as a new chapter for the over 100-year-old studio, in reality it signals the company’s failure to adapt to the digital age.

WBD was itself formed through a $43bn mega-merger in 2022, with bosses saying the deal would give it the scale required to compete in the streaming age. Just three years later, it has split back in two and is being subsumed by its largest streaming rival.

Netflix’s swoop has already ruffled feathers in Hollywood. The companies have said they expect cost savings of up to $3bn a year within three years, sparking fears of significant job cuts.

Jason Kilar, former chief executive of Warner Media, wrote on X: “If I was tasked with doing so, I could not think of a more effective way to reduce competition in Hollywood than selling WBD to Netflix.”

Others fear it could spell the end of traditional cinema, with Netflix shunning theatrical releases in favour of premiering shows on its streaming service.

James Cameron, the director of Titanic and Avatar, said this week that a deal with Netflix would be a “disaster”. Netflix has insisted it will maintain Warner Bros’ current operations and continue theatrical releases for films.

The deal, which will bring together two of the largest streaming services in the US, will likely also face regulatory scrutiny amid concerns it could push up prices for subscribers. As a result, Netflix could be forced to forgo HBO to get the deal over the line.

That is not to mention the potential political influence Trump could exert through the FCC, the US media regulator led by the president’s appointee and acolyte Brendan Carr.

Netflix has said it doesn’t expect the deal to close for 12 to 18 months, suggesting drawn-out regulatory scrutiny is on the cards. It has also agreed to pay a $5.8bn termination fee if the deal fails to materialise.

For Netflix, however, these hurdles are a small price to pay as the streaming giant cements its domination of Hollywood once and for all.

data.title: Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn a year to energy bills
data.description:
The troubled Hinkley Point C nuclear power station will add £1bn annually to UK energy bills as soon as it’s switched on, official figures show.

The money will be taken from consumers and handed to the French owner EDF to subsidise operations, making it one of the UK’s most expensive sources of electricity.

A further £1bn will be added to bills by a separate nuclear levy, supporting construction of the Sizewell C nuclear power station in Suffolk, also led by EDF. Campaigners branded it a “nuclear tax on households”.

Details were revealed in documents released by the Treasury and the Office for Budget Responsibility in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

They describe how EDF will be entitled to claim the money under the “Contracts for Difference” subsidy system as soon as Hinkley C begins operations, probably in 2030.

The documents state: “In 2030-31, Contracts for Difference (CfDs) are expected to generate £4.6bn in government receipts, including £1bn to fund subsidy payments to the Hinkley Point C nuclear power plant for its first year of expected generation.”

The impact on bills is linked to a 2013 agreement reached between EDF and Sir Ed Davey, the then energy secretary. He guaranteed that EDF could charge £92.50 per megawatt hour (MWh) of power once Hinkley Point C came online. With inflation, this equates to £133 today and is expected to reach about £150 in 2030.

If the wholesale cost of electricity remains at its current level of about £80/MWh, then EDF can claim an extra £70 from consumers and businesses via CfDs.

From January, energy bills will also be hit by an entirely separate levy designed to support the construction of another nuclear power station at Sizewell in Suffolk.

The Regulated Asset Base levy will add £10 a year to power bills from 2026, raising £700m, but will roughly double by 2030, when it will need to raise £1.4bn a year for Sizewell.

Official modelling suggests Sizewell C’s final costs could reach £100bn.

Bills could rise even further once approval is given for another nuclear site on Anglesey in north Wales, where the UK’s first small modular reactors are planned.

‘We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax’

Alison Downes, of Stop Sizewell C, said nuclear power had proven to be among the most expensive ways of generating electricity.

She said: “The Government has a misguided belief that nuclear will be a cheap, ‘green’ solution to our energy needs, but the evidence shows the opposite – that costs of delivery and of dealing with nuclear waste – will continue to rise.

“We remain opposed to the imposition of a nuclear tax on households, given the acknowledged uncertainty about the projected costs of constructing Sizewell C.”

The surging cost of CfDs is part of a much broader increase in energy-related environmental levies. These add £14bn to energy bills at the moment, but this will rise to £19bn by the end of the decade, according to Treasury forecasts.

The CfD subsidy system, which also supports wind and solar, currently costs consumers and businesses £2.3bn a year, but those costs will hit £4.6bn by 2030-31.

Meanwhile, the Renewables Obligation, which funded the UK’s first wind farms, adds costs of £8bn a year.

The Capacity Market, which pays gas-fired power stations and hydroelectric facilities to be on standby in case wind and solar are hit by low wind and light levels, adds a further £1.6bn.

A government spokesman said the subsidies were necessary to unleash its planned “golden age of nuclear power”.

They said: “Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C will each deliver clean electricity for six million households for at least six decades. Sizewell C could save £2bn a year across the future low-carbon electricity system once operational, resulting in cheaper power for consumers.”

Hinkley C was initially forecast to cost £18bn, but spiralling construction costs have pushed the total bill to £46bn, equivalent to £700 per person in the UK.

EDF was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: This is the Chancellor’s biggest lie yet
data.description:
Of all the fake news that tumbled out of the Chancellor’s mouth on Budget day, the notion that this Government is about to restore some sense of normality to Britain’s broken energy system is perhaps the biggest lie of all.

Not that your typical family would know any better of course, which is precisely the point. To the vast majority of households, £150 off an annual energy bill is not to be sniffed at, especially during these straitened times.

The move helps to paint a picture of a Labour Government looking out for working people, not to mention honouring a key manifesto pledge to make energy more affordable.

Factor in a further £150 towards your energy costs through the Warm Home Discount for those on benefits and bills might start to look even more manageable for the poorest in the country.

Indeed, add the two together and it is the view of some Labour MPs that the Government will soon be able to claim that it has met its promise to cut energy bills by £300 – at least for the six million or so people that fall into this camp.

It truly is desperate stuff from a party heading for oblivion. The reality is that Labour deserves no applause whatsoever for this cynical attempt to pull the wool over our eyes.

The proposals laid out by Rachel Reeves at the despatch box amount to financial sleight of hand of the very worst – a cynical confidence trick played on unsuspecting voters without the knowledge to spot what is happening.

Yes, there’s a £150 reduction coming, but to pretend that this is some sort of permanent adjustment that means we’re heading to a world where it will be cheaper to heat our homes and turn on the lights is totally disingenuous.

What the Chancellor announced on Wednesday is nothing more than a short-term reprieve from an energy system that has become a dangerous mess and where bills are only going to get more prohibitively expensive.

What’s more, it is a situation that has been largely created by catastrophic policy decisions from a Government that has no idea what it’s doing. So for Labour to be asking everyone to be thankful for “bringing down energy bills” and easing the cost of living – as Reeves claimed straight-faced on Wednesday – really does take some beating.

The Treasury’s £150 saving is nothing more than a ruse designed to make people look the other way, while Labour piles on yet more levies and crushes domestic production with its decimation of the North Sea, sending bills further into the stratosphere in the process.

The basis for the £150 reduction is fairly straightforward once you lay it out. The Government is ditching the so-called energy company obligation, a scheme that forces energy suppliers to fund energy efficiency measures such as home insulation for low-income households. That will save a typical household £67 a year, experts say.

Plus, it was introduced under the Cameron Government, so Labour presumably thinks it gets bonus points for laying some of the current unaffordability at the door of the Opposition.

Then there’s a further £78 annual saving through the partial removal of the renewables obligation – effectively another green levy to pay for the construction of wind farms.

Yet the part that the Chancellor has shamefully chosen to be less forthcoming about is the long list of green levy increases barrelling down the track – increases that are bound to wipe out any savings over the long-term.

Still, at least the Office for Budget Responsibility – fresh from its attempt to torpedo the Budget entirely – is living up to its name and keeping count.

It notes that the cost of the capacity market – which pays for gas fired power stations, battery parks and hydro-electric plants to be on stand-by in the event that wind and solar plants fail – will jump from £1bn to about £4.5bn over the next five years.

As the OBR wonks point out: “Renewable energy sources ... have more variable energy generation compared to non-renewable sources.”

Similarly the costs of Contracts for Difference – yet more subsidies which fund wind and solar plants – will more than double from about £2bn this year to north of £4bn by 2030.

Then there’s the cost of financing Sizewell C in Suffolk, expected to spiral from £300m today to £1.4bn at the end of the decade, plus the Warm Home Discount that will add a further £1.1bn to annual bills.

Add in the estimated £150 rise in bills due to the cost of upgrading the national grid and the picture is much clearer: energy costs are set to go through the roof as a direct result of this Government’s insistence on going hell for leather with renewables.

Again, this is laid out in black and white by the OBR. It calculates that the cost of green taxes on energy bills will surge from £14bn this year to £19bn by 2030 under Reeves’s Budget proposals. Twenty years ago, receipts from environmental levies totalled just £300m.

As the OBR neatly puts it, “this reflects a shift away from the taxation of energy extraction to taxation on the consumption of energy”, so voters should be in no doubt about what is happening here.

It wouldn’t be quite so bad if Labour possessed the foresight to smooth the so-called energy transition with home-grown oil and gas reserves – but instead, the Government has embarked on a wilful destruction of the North Sea at the same time.

Faced with a series of anti-oil and gas policies, including the windfall tax, offshore operators are abandoning the region in their droves in what is set to trigger a near-collapse in tax receipts. Tax revenues from North Sea oil and gas will plummet ninefold from £2.7bn to just £300m between 2025 and 2030, the OBR says.

It also means the UK will become increasingly reliant on more costly imports of fossil fuels from the likes of Norway, Qatar and the US at a time when it already boasts the most expensive electricity prices in the world. The UK grid operator has warned that the amount of gas produced by the North Sea will decline by over 80pc this year alone.

The effect of all this then is to keep bills higher than they need to be at the same time as massively undermining energy security. How any of this makes sense is one of the great puzzles of our time. In the hands of Sir Keir Starmer, Reeves and Ed Miliband, this country is firmly on the path to self-destruction.

data.title: Chip giant caught in Dutch-Chinese row warns carmakers of factory shutdowns
data.description:
A major chip company that was seized by the Dutch government has warned of “imminent production stoppages” at carmakers amid a standoff with its rogue Chinese branch.

Nexperia, which produces semiconductors used across the car industry, said in an open letter to staff at its Chinese division that factories risked shutdown without a reopening of relations.

The Dutch business said it had been “compelled to communicate publicly” after its “repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue” were ignored.

The chipmaker has been at the centre of a geopolitical crisis after Dutch officials invoked a Cold War-era law to allow it to take effective control of the business. The Government justified the move by warning that the company’s Chinese owners, Wingtech, posed a threat to Europe’s chip supplies and accused them of governance failings.

The decision prompted Beijing to impose export controls on Nexperia’s chips, while staff at its Chinese business stopped following instructions from its European headquarters.

The blockade threatened to devastate Europe’s car industry and prompted The Hague to back down. The Dutch government revoked its order earlier this month after negotiations to diffuse the crisis.

Nexperia’s technology is used in dozens of electronic components that feature in modern cars, ranging from electronic window controls to windscreen wipers.

Wingtech’s shares in the business remain under the control of a court-appointed administrator in the Netherlands and its Chinese chief executive has also been suspended.

On Thursday, Nexperia claimed its Chinese branch had continued to ignore attempts to restart normal business despite the detente between Dutch and Chinese governments.

“Nexperia has made repeated and multiple attempts, both formal and informal, to re-establish the dialogue with Nexperia’s entities in China by means of direct outreach via calls, emails and proposed meetings, various offers to collaborate on mutually beneficial solutions and even formal correspondence to demand performance of rights,” the company said.

“Regrettably, Nexperia did not receive any meaningful response. We urge you to respond to Nexperia’s outreaches and engage constructively and with a view to restoring the supply chain, as this is in the interest of all parties involved, including notably our customers.

“In light of this, we are compelled to communicate publicly to underscore the urgency.”

A Wingtech spokesman said Nexperia’s letter was “highly misleading” and said it had “repeatedly expressed our willingness to engage in constructive communication”.

The spokesman said: “We have also, on numerous occasions through multiple channels, reached out to Nexperia to pursue a negotiated solution.

“If the control dispute remains unresolved, numerous customers could face supply chain disruptions.”

Separately on Friday, Wingtech said it had filed an appeal against a court order stripping it of control of Nexperia in the Netherlands with the country’s supreme court.

data.title: Tax on rental income: What is it, calculations and how to pay
data.description:
In the same way that workers must pay tax on their earnings, landlords also have to pay tax on the income they receive from tenants.

From April 2027, this will get even more expensive, as the Government unveiled plans in the 2025 Budget to increase rental income tax by two percentage points.

While buy-to-let investors used to pay tax on rental income “net of mortgage costs”, they can now only claim a 20pc tax credit on their mortgage interest payments, which has eaten into profits.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how tax on rental income is calculated, and what it means if you have multiple properties and rentals overseas.

  • What is tax on rental income?
  • How is tax on rental income calculated?
  • Rental income tax on multiple properties
  • Rental income tax on properties overseas
  • Rental income tax FAQs

What is tax on rental income?

Tax on rental income refers to income tax, which is payable on the money you receive from tenants. The rates, thresholds and tax-free allowances are currently the same as for any other type of income, whether it’s from employment, or a pension income. 

However, from April 2027 rental income will incur higher rates of tax. Basic-rate taxpayers will pay 22pc on rental income, while those who pay higher-rate tax will pay 42pc and it’s 47pc for additional-rate payers.

You can also use certain costs incurred as part of being a landlord to reduce your tax bill – but since the rules changed a few years ago, you can now offset less of your mortgage costs.

Chris Etherington, of accountants RSM UK, said: “For residential lettings, there are special provisions for mortgage and other loan finance costs. Relief for these is given as a reduction in tax, rather than set against rental profits, at a rate of 20pc.”

How is tax on rental income calculated?

Tax on rental income works differently depending on whether you are taxed as an individual, or as a company, if you have incorporated the ownership of the property.

If you are taxed as an individual, the income tax will work in much the same way as the tax you would pay on income earned from employment.

Rachael Griffin, of wealth manager Quilter, said: “It is taxed at 20pc, 40pc or 45pc, depending on your tax bracket. The main difference is that you would need to complete a self-assessment tax return and calculate your profits.”

The amount you need to pay is determined by subtracting any tax-free allowances and expenses from your total rental income – resulting in your taxable profit.

As mentioned above, this profit is then taxed according to your income band.

For the 2025-26 tax year:

  • Basic-rate taxpayers pay 20pc on income between £12,570-£50,270
  • Higher-rate taxpayers pay 40pc on income between £50,271-£125,140
  • Additional-rate taxpayers pay 45pc on income over £125,140.

While most taxpayers pay nothing on earnings up to £12,570 – the personal allowance – this reduces once you earn more than £100,000; for every £2 extra you earn, you lose £1 of the tax-free allowance. By the time you earn £125,140, you’ll have no personal allowance at all.

As the UK has a progressive tax system, be aware that you need to add any rent from letting a property with any other taxable income to determine the tax rate that may apply.

Ms Griffin added: “For example, if you were earning £35,000 from employment and earned a further £20,000 from rental income, you would be pushed into the higher rate of income tax and would therefore pay the higher rate of 40pc on £4,730.”

Why do you pay tax on rental income?

  • Rental income is considered taxable earnings, like a salary or business profit
  • Paying tax on rental income is a legal obligation
  • While other investments can benefit from being held in an Isa (individual savings account) where growth is tax-free, the Government does not incentivise investing in property in the same way
  • Even though the property tax rules have been put together by different governments over time, a key reason for the current tax rules could be due to the lack of housing supply. The rules in place at present could potentially deter many would-be landlords from starting – or continuing – rental businesses, in theory increasing the number of properties available for owner-occupiers.

Rental income tax on multiple properties

If you have multiple properties, you will typically need to pool together all of the profits and treat them as income from one business. You then deduct allowable expenses for each property to calculate your total taxable profit.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For example, if one generates a profit and another operates at a loss, the loss from one property can offset the profit from another – potentially reducing your overall tax liability.”

To make this process easier, it is highly advisable to keep separate, detailed records for each property, covering both income and expenses.

Mr Mendes added: “This ensures accuracy and simplifies the process of filing your tax return.”

Note though, that there are some exceptions to the guidelines set out above.

Mr Etherington said: “This includes rental income from properties held in partnership which would need to be declared as a separate rental business.”

Rental income tax on properties overseas

Be aware that profits from an overseas rental property can still be taxable in the UK, even if tax has been paid overseas as well.

Mr Mendes said: “Owning a property overseas adds an extra layer of complexity to your tax obligation. Fortunately, the UK has double taxation agreements with many countries, allowing you to claim relief and avoid being taxed twice on the same income.”

But as navigating the specific tax rules in both jurisdictions can be challenging, it’s worth seeking professional advice to ensure compliance – and also to make sure you don’t end up overpaying on tax.

When to pay rental income tax

The following are the deadlines for the 2024-25 return:

  • 31 October 2025: paper Self Assessment return deadline
  • 30 December 2025: deadline to submit online if you want HMRC to collect tax through PAYE code (for under £3,000 owed)
  • 31 January 2026: deadline for online Self Assessment return and balancing payment for 2024–25, plus first payment on account towards 2025–26
  • 31 July 2026: second payment on account for 2025–26

Failure to declare rental income can result in fines, penalties and in some cases legal action. By staying compliant, you can ensure your tax affairs are in order.

If you let a property and earn income from it, take care to stay within the rules. You will need to calculate profits and file a self-assessment tax return.

Expenses and tax allowances

Expenses:

Allowable expenses are costs which are incurred “wholly and exclusively” for the purposes of renting out a property. They can be deducted from your rental income to reduce your taxable profit. These include:

  • Utility bills
  • Property repairs
  • Service charge costs
  • Letting agent fees
  • Landlord insurance.

Ms Griffin said: “These expenses can help to reduce your taxable income when completing your self-assessment tax return, so it is important to keep a detailed log of all expenses.”

Allowances:

There are a host of property-related tax-free allowances for individual landlords. These include:

  • the property allowance, which can exempt up to £1,000 of property income a year from income tax
  • the rent-a-room scheme, which can allow an owner-occupier to receive up to £7,500 of rental income a year tax-free – provided it is received from a tenant living in their home.

Tax on rental income FAQs

Yes, you still pay tax on rental income, even if you’re paying off a mortgage.

Having a mortgage on your rental property can significantly affect how much tax you pay.

Previously, landlords could deduct the full amount of mortgage interest from their rental income, but in recent years, tax relief for landlords has been reduced.

Mr Mendes said: “Now, if you let a property, you can only claim a 20pc tax credit on your mortgage interest payments.”

This can mean that landlords who are higher-rate or additional-rate taxpayers will not receive full tax relief on their rental income.

Mr Etherington warned: “In some instances, landlords can make a loss on their rental business but still end up with a tax liability.”

This depends on your other income. If, as a landlord, you don’t have any other earnings – other than rental income – you might have no income tax to pay. This is because the rent you get may fall within your personal allowance. This allows you to earn a certain amount of income tax-free.

Most individuals get a personal allowance of £12,570 (frozen at this level until April 6 2028) – and will not usually have a tax liability if their taxable income is below this.

Additionally, landlords may benefit from the £1,000 property allowance. This can be used as an alternative to claiming actual expenses if it is more beneficial.

Yes. Rental income is taxable, even if it is your only source of income.

That said, as mentioned above, if your total income – including rental income – is below the personal allowance (of £12,570 for the 2025-26 tax year), you won’t owe any tax.

Mr Mendes said: “Despite this, you are still required to declare the income to HMRC to ensure your records are accurate and up to date. Equally, if your rental income does exceed the personal allowance, then you will be required to pay tax on the amount above the threshold.”

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: US streaming giants are taking cash-strapped Britons for granted
data.description:
If you are looking forward to watching A Merry Little Ex-Mas, starring Alicia Silverstone as a divorced woman trying to host a perfect family holiday before her ex-husband arrives with his new girlfriend; or My Secret Santa, about a single mother trying to get a job as a ski resort Father Christmas – and let’s be honest here, who could possibly want to miss that – then there is some bad news.

The Netflix subscription required to treat yourself to those films is more expensive than last year. Likewise, the Spotify sub to listen to the Jingle Bells playlist – or put All I Want For Christmas Is You on repeat – will cost more as well.

The streaming giants have discovered the secret to making lots of money. They have hooked us all on their services and so they push up the prices. 

Yet they can’t keep that up forever – because eventually the customers are going to get tired of it.

The days of cheap streaming are now long in the past. It emerged last week that Spotify is planning to raise its prices in the crucial US market early in the new year, after already raising the price in Britain, Switzerland and Australia.

What started as a $9.99 (£7.55) a month deal for listening to every piece of music ever made has already gone up to $11.99 and looks set to climb higher. It is not hard to understand why.

JP Morgan analysts estimate that even a $1-a-month increase in the US will add $500m a year to the company’s revenue. Goldman Sachs estimated in September that the music streamers, which have so far been relatively restrained compared with television, would increase prices every 12 to 24 months from now on.

Likewise, Netflix increased its standard monthly package by $2.50 in the US this year and in the UK by £2 a month. It has also started showing ads to boost revenues and you have to pay extra to skip them. Apple TV put up its prices by 30pc in the summer and Disney+ by $3 a month in October.

In the background, the streaming services are consolidating.

ITV could get sold to Sky, now owned by the American giant Comcast – and that would include its streaming service as well as its terrestrial channel. Paramount Global is in the process of being sold to David Ellison’s emerging Skydance conglomerate, while there are reports of a mega-merger between Paramount and Warner Bros to create a genuine rival to Netflix.

There are going to be fewer streaming services but each one will have a lock on its market and may well feel it can keep on pushing up prices.

Of course, we can all understand that is very good for business. Streaming is a great industry if you get it right. True, Spotify shares some of its revenues with the record labels – and even occasionally with the artists – and the movie deals can be complex.

But as a rule, if you can make a price increase stick, the extra revenues drop straight down to the bottom line.

Spotify shares are up 30pc this year, with the increase driven as much by the prospect of price rises as by new subscribers. Netflix shares are up by 20pc since the start of the year, an impressive result for a fairly mature business. All the evidence suggests those price rises can be made to stick. We are hooked on the service and it will take a lot to persuade anyone to give it up now.

The trouble is, there are two big problems with the attempt to constantly squeeze more and more money out of a captive audience. First, consumers are becoming exhausted.

Sure, at first an extra pound or dollar does not matter very much. The subscription typically gets taken straight out of your bank account and it might be a while before you even notice.

Yet that will not be true forever. With a stagnant economy in the UK, with inflation rising and with taxes going up all the time, at some point people will turn off in droves. Once they have gone, it will be very hard to get them back again.

Next, it opens up the market for lower-cost competitors. In television, the Fox-owned Tubi offers ad-supported broadcasting. YouTube is an alternative to Spotify for anyone who gets tired of the price rises.

There are already platforms that allow artists to sell their music directly to their fans and they may grow in popularity, while sports teams may find it makes more sense financially to cut out the middlemen.

The important point is this. Once your TV or speakers are hooked up to the internet, there are lots of different ways of receiving content and entrepreneurs will constantly experiment with new ways of making that work.

The more expensive the existing giants become, the more tempting some of those rivals will look – and once they get started, they may be able to expand very quickly.

In reality, the streamers are taking the market for granted. They are hoping that a handful of giants will have such a grip on consumers that they can do whatever they want. Netflix is certainly already in that position and so is Amazon’s Prime service, and perhaps Disney as well. So are Spotify and Apple Music.

Their competitors have either fallen by the wayside or else they are furiously merging with one another so that they can join the elite before it is too late. Investors are betting that they can keep on effortlessly pushing up the cost of a subscription and make more and more money. Perhaps they can.

Yet that is one of the riskiest business strategies of all time. Eventually, the customer will get fed up and someone will work out how to offer a cheaper service – and then the streaming giants will find that price does matter after all.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Netflix lands Harry Potter franchise with $83bn Warner Bros takeover
data.description:
Netflix has inked an $83bn (£62bn) deal to take control of the studios behind hits such as Harry Potter and Succession.

The US subscription business has entered a definitive agreement to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery’s (WBD) studio and streaming businesses after emerging victorious in a hotly contested bidding war.

The deal will give the US streaming platform access to a vast library of films and TV shows to beef up its offering to users.

WBD owns the historic Hollywood studio behind franchises such as Harry Potter and Batman, as well as the HBO Max streaming service, which is home to hits such as Succession and White Lotus.

However, the proposed merger has already faced opposition from high-profile figures in Hollywood.

James Cameron, the director of Titanic and Avatar, said this week that a deal with Netflix would be a “disaster” as it poses a threat to the future of traditional theatrical film releases.

The merger is also expected to lead to significant job cuts, with the companies confirming they expect cost savings of up to $3bn a year within three years.

Any deal would mark a massive reshaping of Hollywood and cement Netflix’s dominance after emerging victorious in the streaming wars.

Greg Peters, co-chief executive of Netflix, said: “Warner Bros. has helped define entertainment for more than a century and continues to do so with phenomenal creative executives and production capabilities.

“With our global reach and proven business model, we can introduce a broader audience to the worlds they create - giving our members more options, attracting more fans to our best-in-class streaming service, strengthening the entire entertainment industry and creating more value for shareholders.”

David Zaslav, chief executive of WBD, said: “Today’s announcement combines two of the greatest storytelling companies in the world to bring to even more people the entertainment they love to watch the most.

“For more than a century, Warner Bros. has thrilled audiences, captured the world’s attention, and shaped our culture. By coming together with Netflix, we will ensure people everywhere will continue to enjoy the world’s most resonant stories for generations to come.”

Netflix’s cash-and-share offer is valued at $27.75 per share, implying a total enterprise value of $82.7bn.

The deal is not expected to close for at least a year. WBD will first complete a separate transaction to spin off its networks division, which includes CNN, TNT Sports in the US and Discovery.

The Hollywood giant had previously announced plans to split into two before takeover interest prompted it to confirm it was up for sale, starting the gun on a bidding war.

The auction also attracted interest from Paramount, the Hollywood studio recently acquired by the billionaire Ellison family, and cable giant Comcast.

Comcast, which owns Sky in the UK, is also thought to have bid for just the studio and streaming business. However, the US conglomerate is also in talks over a £1.6bn takeover of ITV’s broadcasting business.

Sky is already gearing up for the loss of exclusive rights to HBO shows in the UK when the HBO Max streaming service launches in the UK in March. The merger means the broadcaster will be facing even stronger competition as Netflix and HBO Max are likely to be combined into one major streaming rival.

As for WBD, Paramount had been viewed as the frontrunner, having tabled an offer for the entire business, including cable networks such as CNN.

Close ties between the Ellison family and Donald Trump were also seen as an advantage for the company.

However, Paramount last night lashed out at the sale process, accusing WBD bosses of favouring Netflix’s bid.

In a letter to Mr Zaslav, lawyers for Paramount wrote: “It has become increasingly clear, through media reporting and otherwise, that WBD appears to have abandoned the semblance and reality of a fair transaction process, thereby abdicating its duties to stockholders, and embarked on a myopic process with a predetermined outcome that favours a single bidder.”

In response, WBD said: “Please be assured that the WBD board attends to its fiduciary obligations with the utmost care and that they have fully and robustly complied with them and will continue to do so.”

Any tie-up between the two companies is likely to face regulatory scrutiny, as it would combine two of the largest streaming services in the US.

data.title: What is a cash Isa, and how does one work?
data.description:
If you have money to save, then a cash Isa should be one of your considerations. It works in a similar way to a savings account, except that you’re limited to how much you can pay in each year, and the interest your money earns will remain tax-free.

Some changes are coming for cash Isa savers in April 2027. Under government plans announced in the 2025 autumn Budget, only over-65s will be able to continue saving up to £20,000 of their Isa allowance into a cash account. Younger savers will have their cash Isa limit reduced to £12,000, and the remaining £8,000 must be invested instead.

There are several types of cash Isas to choose from, and they’re offered by lots of banks and building societies, as well as National Savings & Investments.

If you’re not sure quite where to start, Telegraph Money will explain the basics of cash Isas in this guide, which will cover:

  • What is a cash Isa?
  • Benefits of a cash Isa
  • Types of cash Isas available
  • How to make cash Isas work for you
  • Cash Isa FAQs

What is a cash Isa?

A cash Isa is an individual savings account that allows you to earn interest on your savings without paying tax on the interest earned.

Cash Isas were launched in 1999 with the aim of helping savers reduce their tax bills.

While they went out of fashion when savings rates were at record lows – as there was little risk of savers earning enough in interest to trigger a charge – the higher rates we’ve seen over the last couple of years mean they have a more important role to play.

Savings rates reached upwards of 6pc in 2023, and it’s still possible to find deals above 4.5pc now, which means more people are breaching their “personal savings allowance” (more on this below), and are therefore at risk of having to pay tax on their interest.

If you’re trying to keep some of your cash out of the taxman’s clutches – not just now, but years in the future – saving tax-free in an Isa is a sensible move.

Who can open a cash Isa?

In order to open a cash Isa in the UK, you must:

  • Be aged 18 or over,
  • Be a UK resident for tax purposes, including Crown servants and their spouses serving overseas,
  • Have a National Insurance number, which is needed to apply for an Isa.

You can also open a junior Isa for your children if they are under 18. Discover the best junior cash Isa deals and rates in our dedicated article.

Benefits of a cash Isa

There are some key benefits to a cash Isa over a regular savings account:

  • Cash Isas currently allow you to pay in up to £20,000 a year, and earn interest free of income tax
  • They are separate from the Personal Savings Allowance (PSA)
  • Savings held within a cash Isa are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) up to £85,000
  • Cash Isas are flexible and easy to manage.

By sheltering money in a cash Isa you can keep more of the interest you earn in your own hands – and cash Isas are tax-free for life. As your savings grow, building with compound interest, an Isa means you can shelter more and more interest from the tax man.”

What’s more, cash Isas are easy to set up, and simple to operate.

Laith Khalaf, from AJ Bell, said: “These vehicles are good for storing money that you might need at the drop of a hat. Unlike a stocks and shares Isa, the value of your cash Isa will never fall.”

In this article, we focus on cash Isas, but you can read our comprehensive guide on how Isas work for more information about other account types.

Types of cash Isa

There are several different types of cash Isa on the market, including:

  • Fixed-rate cash Isas
  • Variable-rate cash Isas
  • Notice cash Isas
  • Regular cash Isas
  • Lifetime Isas
  • Junior cash Isas

Fixed-rate cash Isas

If you are happy to lock your money away, better rates are likely to be available on fixed-rate Isas, compared to variable cash Isas. However, they require you to commit to locking your money away for a set period of time, most commonly between one and five years.

If you need to withdraw your money before the end of the fixed term you’re likely to face a penalty – often, providers will charge you a certain amount of interest on the sum you’re withdrawing.

Variable-rate cash Isas

One common type on variable-rate account is an “easy-access” Isa – which usually allows you to get your hands on your money whenever you need it, penalty-free. That being said, individual accounts may have their own more restrictive withdrawal rules.

The main drawback to this kind of cash Isa is that rates can change at any time – so the rate of interest you sign up for could reduce in future. 

You also need to keep a close eye for any bonus rates, which may bump up the interest rate when you first open the account only to drop some time afterwards. If there is one, make a note to move your money to a more competitive deal once the bonus ends.

Notice cash Isas

Isas with a notice period tend to have slightly better rates than easy-access accounts (but this isn’t always the case). In theory, the longer the notice period, the higher the rate of interest is likely to be.

Be aware that with this type of Isa you can usually make unlimited withdrawals, but you must give a certain number of days’ notice before you can withdraw your funds. This can vary, but is most commonly between 30 days to 180 days. You need to bear this in mind if you’re likely to need faster access to your cash.

Regular cash Isas

Specific terms will vary, but these accounts commonly have a set amount that you can pay in each month – sometimes a certain number of deposits are required in order to get the advertised interest rate, such as 10 per year, or one every calendar month. You might be able to make withdrawals, but those offering a higher rate might not allow them for a set period.

Note that the advertised interest rate might sound high, but as you’re usually restricted to gradually paying in relatively low amounts, the actual interest you’ll earn will be fairly low.

Lifetime Isas

Technically, lifetime Isas are their own separate type of Isa – they have a different deposit limit (£4,000), and the money is meant to fund either your first home or retirement. However, we are mentioning them here since there are cash Isa options. You can find out more in our guide to Lisas.

Junior cash Isas

For children under the age of 18, parents or guardians can open a Junior Isa for them – there are cash and stocks and shares Isas available.

How to make cash Isas work for you

There are several steps to making the most of a cash Isa:

1. Work out if a cash Isa is the best option

There are certain savers who would do well to consider a cash Isa – usually those who need to retain access to their money. Mr Khalaf said: “Cash Isas are well suited to those building up a rainy day fund, and those saving for short-term spending needs – with the money needed in the next five years or so.”

2. Decide which type suits you

The key for this decision is working out how much flexibility you’ll need from your account – can you leave the money untouched for a year or two, or do you need to make withdrawals often? This can help you identify whether an easy-access Isa is best, or a fixed-term (which usually has higher rates). 

Of course, you don’t just have to choose one – you can split up your cash into different accounts, so you can retain access to some and lock up the rest. Our guide to the savings laddering tactic reveals how to split your savings to secure the highest rates year after year.

3. Find the best rates

With inflation remaining higher than the Bank of England’s 2pc target, price rises will be eroding the real terms buying power of your savings – therefore, it’s important to secure the best interest rate possible. 

Telegraph Money’s guide to the best cash Isas highlights the top rates across all account types, updated daily.

4. Act quickly to secure the best deals

When you’ve found a cash Isa that’s right for you it’s worth getting your skates on, as the top deals may not hang around for long. Rachel Springall, finance expert at moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Offers can change quickly, so speed is crucial to grab a top rate.”

5. Regularly review your account

After tucking money away in a cash Isa it’s key to review it regularly to ensure it’s still paying a competitive rate – particularly if it has a variable rate that can change at any time.

If you’ve signed up to a fixed-rate deal, make a note of when it finishes and set about finding a new home for your money – otherwise, your provider may move it to a low-rate easy-access account.

Understanding the tax benefits

One of the biggest benefits of a cash Isa is the fact that this vehicle protects your savings from tax.

As a saver, you need to take care not to breach your “personal savings allowance”. This is the limit on interest you can earn tax-free, and applies to the interest you earn across all accounts.

Your allowance depends on your tax band – i.e. the highest rate of income tax you pay. It is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer, and just £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. Additional-rate taxpayers pay tax on all their interest.

A consequence of income tax thresholds being frozen until at least 2030-31, as announced in the latest Budget, more people will be dragged into higher tax bands, and will therefore be more likely to breach their personal savings allowance. This is when sheltering your hard-earned savings in a cash Isa becomes so much more important.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no rule about how much money you can hold in a cash Isa – the restriction is just on how much you can pay in each tax year.

Under current rules, the annual Isa allowance is £20,000. You can opt to put up to £20,000 in one Isa, or split it among several. This £20,000 allowance has not changed since 2017.

From April 2027, there are plans to restrict the cash Isa allowance to £12,000 for savers aged under 65. They will still have an overall £20,000 Isa allowance, but the remaining £8,000 will need to be invested, rather than saved in cash. Those aged over 65 won’t be affected by these changes.

Note that if you don’t use your Isa allowance, you lose it, as it cannot be carried over to the next tax year.

Yes. If you have a cash Isa, you are allowed to move your money between providers at any time – and you can also move it to a different type of Isa, as long as the provider accepts transfers. But you must follow the official Isa transfer rules to ensure the funds remain under the protection of the tax-free wrapper.

If you withdraw money from your cash Isa to your bank account before putting it into a new cash Isa, your hard-earned savings will lose their tax-free status while they’re not held in an Isa, and the deposit will also be deducted from your Isa allowance. Transferring existing Isa savings directly between providers won’t incur these problems.

Ms Springall said: “You must ensure you transfer the cash in accordance with the rules to keep its tax-free wrapper. But note that not every cash Isa will allow transfers in from cash Isas – or stocks and shares Isas.”

Be sure to check this ahead of making any move.

In any one tax year, you can make as many transfers as you wish. Also note that following new rules which kicked in from April 6 2024 you can now make partial transfers of Isas.

The Government has announced plans to restrict transfers from stocks and shares Isas to cash Isas from April 2027, in a bid to stop savers circumventing the reduced cash Isa limit – full details on this are yet to be announced.

Rules state that when you go overseas you are no longer allowed to pay into your Isa after the end of the tax year that you move.

Beyond this date, for tax purposes, you are no longer a British resident and will not be eligible for an Isa. The only exception is if you are a Crown employee working abroad, such as a diplomat, civil servant or member of the armed forces.

You need to let your Isa provider as soon as you stop being a British resident.

The good news is that even if you no longer live in the UK, any existing Isas you have will remain open and active and you will be able to go on benefiting from tax relief on any cash held in your accounts. You are also still allowed to transfer your Isa to another provider.

Note though that the tax rules may be different in the country you’ve moved to, and that you could be required to pay tax in your new country on the money earned from your Isa – you need to check this.

If you do return to the UK and become a British resident once again, you will be able to start paying into your Isa (subject to the annual £20,000 allowance).

data.title: Canary Wharf revival gathers pace as Visa prepares to relocate European HQ
data.description:
Visa has struck a deal to move its European headquarters to London’s Canary Wharf, handing a fresh boost to the city’s financial district.

The US payments company has agreed to lease 300,000 sq ft on a 15-year deal at One Canada Square, relocating from its current base in Paddington in the summer of 2028.

The agreement is a boost for the Canary Wharf Group, which has been battling to regain its status after an exodus of companies in recent years.

A revival of the business district has been gathering momentum in recent weeks, with JP Morgan recently announcing plans to build a £3bn new headquarters there.

The US banking giant said it would build a three million sq ft office that will hold 12,000 people and serve as its main European base.

Shobi Khan, chief executive of Canary Wharf Group, said Visa’s move will mean the firm “joins a fintech community of more than 65 companies”.

It marks a reversal of fortunes for Canary Wharf after the likes of ratings agency Moody’s and magic circle law firm Clifford Chance ditched the district in favour of moves to the City of London.

However, the new string of deals shows the trend might be easing.

In August, Spanish lender BBVA signed a lease to take 250,000 sq ft in Canary Wharf, while Hershey’s, the American chocolate manufacturer, and Zopa, the digital bank, moved to the financial district this year.

Canary Wharf Group, which is co-owned by the Qatar Investment Authority and Canadian private equity firm Brookfield, said more than 750,000 sq ft of office transactions had been announced so far this year, making 2025 its most successful year in more than a decade.

In an attempt to make the district more attractive to potential tenants, Canary Wharf Group has expanded the area’s retail, leisure and hospitality offering. It has also increased the number of residential units and hotels in the area.

Mr Khan said Canary Wharf had been evolving to become “a thriving mixed-use district that supports customers at every stage of their journey”.

data.title: Pay attention to Miss Universe – it’s where the real action is
data.description:
It’s odd to reflect it was perfectly normal for Britons in the 1970s to sit round the TV watching beauty pageants presented by avuncular chaps like Michael Aspel. Little girls like me wondered if our short, plump legs would one day magically transform into the coltish limbs of a glamazon.

Miss Universe never took off here to the extent as Miss World, which was screened on ITV. On top of that, my friends and I found the title misleading. Where was Miss Mars and bright green beauties with tentacles? Before I could raise a petition, BBC1’s controller Michael Grade took beauty shows off the corporation’s screens in 1985. ITV eventually dropped Miss World in 1988.

This, however, may have been a mistake, as few things entertain like watching a beauty contest descend into taloned flounce-outs and hair-tugging. This year’s Miss Universe contest in Thailand has had drama on a par with Dynasty in its heyday.

For those who missed it, here’s a swift canter through the shenanigans, which included two judges resigning before events had properly got going. French-Lebanese composer Omar Harfouch expressed “profound confusion and concern” on discovering that 30 finalists appeared to have been preselected – from 136 contestants – without the official judges being consulted. The claim was denied by organisers, but didn’t stop former French footballer Claude Makélélé stepping down too for “personal reasons”. Then Miss Jamaica fell and was stretchered off to hospital, while Miss Israel was accused of giving “side-eye” to Miss Palestine, though this was probably due to an unfortunate camera angle.

These were just the amuse-bouches before Thai media personality Nawat Itsaragrisil (newly-appointed executive director of Miss Universe) was caught on camera berating Miss Mexico, Fatima Bosch, for “disrespecting” the host country by not feverishly posting about the contest on social media.

Bosch says Nawat called her a “dumbhead”, he protested he merely said she’d “done damage”. Either way, Bosch wasn’t having any of it and stalked out followed by a posse of co-contestants, while Nowat yelled, “Stop! Stop! Security!” Bosch swiftly garnered support from MUA (Miss Universe Association) President Raúl Rocha, who also hails from Mexico. He denounced Nawat’s “malicious” behaviour as intimidating “a defenceless woman”.

Bosch appeared far from cowed. She proceeded to carry off Darth Vader’s Galactic Miss Universe title, spending the next few days issuing statements about female empowerment: “I came here to be a voice for all the women and all the girls who fight for causes.” Rocha backed her up, saying Miss Universe “is an empowerment platform for women”.

His feminist virtue-signalling might have been more impactful if Mexican news outlet El Reforma hadn’t reported that a warrant had been issued against Rocha for trafficking drugs, arms and fuel between Mexico and Guatemala. It was enough to make you yearn for the good old days when Donald Trump owned the franchise and introduced beauty queens to the likes of Jeffrey Epstein and… Oh, hang on a moment.

The fact is that beauty contests have long felt like a way for suspect businessmen to meet gorgeous young women escaping ordinary backgrounds. You don’t get the daughters of dukes entering these pageants, no matter how goddess-like.

But the debacle also reminds us that Miss Universe is alive and thriving, despite disappearing from terrestrial TV in Britain. In fact, the 74th staging of Miss Universe was the most-watched Spanish language programme of 2025, viewed by an “unprecedented” 2.6 billion people across social media in countries like (surprise, surprise) Mexico, the Philippines, Puerto Rico and Venezuela.

The last country even has its own “beauty factory” programme to prepare contenders for pageant life, resulting in more “Big Four” titles (Miss Universe, Miss World, Miss International and Miss Earth) than any other country.

It’s easy to dismiss Miss Universe, but it’s big, big business and tells us much about geopolitics – rather like our very own Eurovision Song Contest. South American nations favour Latin beauty queens in much the same way the Baltic States vote for one another in Eurovision, then award “nil points” to Britain.

Miss Africa and Oceana, Olivia Yacé, resigned post-competition after protesting that African women weren’t being “heard”. Meanwhile, the all-conquering Bosch couldn’t stop issuing girl-power statements like: “When one woman raises her voice, we all raise our voices.” Try telling that to the women of Afghanistan.

But the platitudes are designed to distract us from the men pulling the levers – deciding who lands the real prize of a TV goldmine.

In August, Spanish lender BBVA signed a lease to take 250,000 sq ft in Canary Wharf, while Hershey’s, the American chocolate manufacturer, and Zopa, the digital bank, moved to the financial district this year.

Canary Wharf Group, which is co-owned by the Qatar Investment Authority and Canadian private equity firm Brookfield, said more than 750,000 sq ft of office transactions had been announced so far this year, making 2025 its most successful year in more than a decade.

In an attempt to make the district more attractive to potential tenants, Canary Wharf Group has expanded the area’s retail, leisure and hospitality offering. It has also increased the number of residential units and hotels in the area.

Mr Khan said Canary Wharf had been evolving to become “a thriving mixed-use district that supports customers at every stage of their journey”.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”
