data.title: Sports Direct boss attacks ‘shambolic’ Budget
data.description:
The chief executive of Sports Direct owner Frasers has blamed Rachel Reeves’s “shambolic” Budget for scaring off shoppers.

Michael Murray said the uncertainty leading up to the Budget hammered consumer confidence, which led to households “battening down the hatches”.

Given the scale of speculation regarding potential tax rises, Mr Murray said he “did not blame” shoppers for cutting back.

It comes after the build-up to the Chancellor’s Budget was dominated by a series of leaks, including a mooted increase in income tax that was later abandoned in favour of a decision to freeze tax thresholds for an additional three years.

Mr Murray said: “It was a shambolic run-up to the Budget, all those kites being flown and pulled down, the headlines all over the place.

“It doesn’t give the consumer confidence, despite there being savings in people’s accounts to go out and spend. It didn’t help that the Budget got pushed back because that elongated the confusion.”

Mr Murray said this was still weighing on shopper confidence, with customers being “very selective” on what they were buying. This meant more people were buying last season’s products or cheaper ranges, rather than the latest designs.

He added: “There’s been a lot of inflation out there and price increases on products.”

It comes amid growing anger from high street bosses over the Budget.

Retail chiefs have accused the Chancellor of failing to deliver on a pledge to support the high street following a shake-up of property taxes.

Ms Reeves previously promised an overhaul of business rates would level the playing field “between the high street and online giants”, hitting warehouses and reducing tax bills for bricks-and-mortar stores.

However, independent stores have warned they expect to pay more under changes, which include amendments to how business rates are calculated.

The Frasers chief said business rates at some of its sites were going up by 100pc, adding: “It just doesn’t seem fair or equitable when there’s no incentive to invest in the high street. They should be offering holidays or rate reductions for investment. There’s none of that.”

He said the Chancellor’s decision to increase the national minimum wage would also push up costs, coming on top of last year’s rise and the changes in National Insurance from last April.

At the Budget, Ms Reeves said minimum wages for 18 to 20-year-olds would increase by 8.5pc from next April, from £10 to £10.85 – more than double the rate of inflation.

Mr Murray said wages had risen by more than 20pc over the past three years, adding: “The market is definitely not growing by 20pc, so it’s becoming very difficult.”

It comes amid growing frustration over a lack of engagement from the Government. Mr Murray said ministers were not “reaching out and consulting, so it’s very difficult to find a forum to give a message to the Government”.

He added: “They don’t seem to include large retailers in any of their discussions.”

data.title: Ed Miliband attacks ‘ignorant’ SNP for blocking nuclear power
data.description:
Ed Miliband has attacked SNP leaders’ “total ignorance” in banning new nuclear power plants in Scotland as he urged communities to summon their “inner Yimby”.

The Energy Secretary said on Thursday there should be a debate about the Scottish Government’s existing policy, which he argued was stopping the country from sharing in the creation of jobs and prosperity.

A UK-wide search is under way for sites that can host large-scale nuclear power stations, with Mr Miliband ordering officials to include Scotland – despite the ban imposed by the SNP-run devolved administration.

His comments come as the parties gear up for the Scottish parliamentary elections in May. Labour is currently trailing the SNP, the Greens and the Liberal Democrats in the polls.

Mr Miliband told a nuclear industry event in London: “We need to have the discussion. And I’m sure the Scottish election will frame a discussion on this, which is, ‘Why should Scotland be excluded from the benefits of new nuclear?’

“It makes no sense for Scotland, it makes no sense for the United Kingdom as a whole.

“In the end, these are going to be decisions for a Scottish Government. But we want to maximise the opportunities for the whole of the United Kingdom.

“So why would we turn our backs on an industry – where, right across the world, people are embracing it – for reasons, frankly, of total dogma and total ignorance?”

There are more than 98,000 jobs in the UK’s civil nuclear supply chain, including power station operators, construction firms, fuels, decommissioning and waste management, according to the Nuclear Industry Association (NIA).

Around 5,400 of these jobs are in Scotland.

But with Torness, the country’s last remaining nuclear power station, scheduled to close by the end of this decade, that figure is expected to dwindle over time as efforts switch to decommissioning.

By comparison, two new large-scale plants are under construction in England – Hinkley Point C in Somerset and Sizewell C in Suffolk – with the Government also preparing to build the first small modular reactors (SMRs) with Rolls-Royce on the island of Anglesey in North Wales.

At the peak of construction, the SMR development alone is expected to create up to 3,000 jobs.

The siting review for large nuclear plants is expected to report back in the second half of next year.

Mr Miliband’s remarks follow the publication of a landmark report led by John Fingleton, an economist, which recommended “radical” reforms to speed up the process of approving and building plants.

In his speech, the Energy Secretary hailed the proposals and said the Government believed that “you can’t have energy sovereignty in abundance without making it easier to build things in this country”.

He added: “This Government is willing to have that discussion with the public. We’re willing to have the discussion across renewables, and we’re willing to have it in nuclear as well.

“The need for homegrown clean energy brings out one’s inner Yimby [yes in my backyard]. I can feel my inner Yimby coming out.

“We shouldn’t accept the stagnation and delay that is all too often built into our planning system.”

He also cleared the way for a new wave of privately funded nuclear projects to come forward, promising that the Government would shortly publish guidance for how these could be supported.

Centrica, the British Gas owner, and Amazon-backed X-Energy are among those vying to build their own fleets of mini reactors across Britain, underpinned by a deal between Sir Keir Starmer and Donald Trump that aims to boost transatlantic cooperation on design approvals.

The companies and others are pushing for a relaxation of siting rules to allow construction closer to built-up areas, as well as potentially contentious proposals that could see consumers fund the plants upfront through their bills.

Some nuclear developers including Newcleo have previously scrapped plans for Britain after warnings that the Government was taking too long to set out a path for such private projects.

However, Mr Miliband insisted: “We are really serious about these privately led routes to market, and that’s what our framework will provide.

“Speed really matters. We don’t want to hang around. There is a global race on, and we want to win the global race.”

Gillian Martin, the Scottish energy secretary, said: “The Scottish Government is focussed on supporting growth and creating jobs by capitalising on Scotland’s immense renewable energy capacity rather than expensive new nuclear energy which takes decades to build and creates a legacy of radioactive waste.

“Significant growth in renewables is providing key opportunities for our future energy workforce in Scotland, with independent scenarios from Ernst and Young showing that with the right support, Scotland’s low carbon and renewable energy sector could support nearly 80,000 jobs by 2050.”

data.title: Britain’s Victorian railway embankments are collapsing. And it could cost us billions
data.description:
When a London-bound express train derailed near the village of Shap in Cumbria earlier this month after ploughing into a landslide, ministers insisted that such incidents were vanishingly rare.

What they failed to mention was that landslips on the railway have reached an all-time high, as a stormier and rainier climate threatens to damage hundreds of miles of embankments dating back to Victorian times.

With Britain’s trains busier than ever, fears are now growing that these centuries-old railway embankments cutting through the countryside could pose a serious risk to passengers – and cost billions to make safer.

Britain’s creaking railways have many problems – from overcrowding and late timetables to poor Wi-Fi and constant signal failures. Yet the dangers posed by landslides and poorly maintained embankments can have fatal consequences.

In 2020, three people died in Stonehaven, Scotland when the lead carriages of a high-speed train en route to Aberdeen plunged down a bank after hitting a landslide triggered by torrential rain.

The accident was the first to kill a passenger since 2007 and the first fatal crash caused by a train hitting a landslip since 1995.

Although last month’s Cumbria derailment was the first major landslide incident since Stonehaven, there are concerns that Britain’s railway system is poorly equipped to detect landslides and prevent further derailments.

Alarmingly, only a minority of landslides are detected by monitors set up to provide a warning when they occur.

Just 8pc of landslips – 13 of 147 – were detected via remote sensors last year, despite £33m having been spent on monitors, a freedom of information request to the Office of Rail and Road found.

In the case of the Cumbria derailment, the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) has said that one of its chief concerns was the “effectiveness of on-site monitoring equipment”.

The spike in incidents and low detection rates has begun to raise concerns about whether remote monitoring can protect passengers – and the costs of upgrading the system if not.

Currently, the vast majority of incidents are reported by train drivers, maintenance crews and engineers.

And drivers are becoming increasingly concerned about the growing risks from landslides, some railway chiefs say.

Tricia Williams, managing director of Northern, Britain’s second-biggest train operator, said after Cumbria she was forced to reassure her drivers and train crew that it was safe for services to operate across the north of England.

Ms Williams said Northern had suffered its own near miss at Grange-over-Sands in Cumbria when the track collapsed ahead of a train as an embankment was washed away.

“We have to recognise it’s a risk, but we’ve got to keep the railway moving. The key is the monitoring equipment,” she said.

“I think there was a monitor at Shap, but there needs to be a certain number of monitors triggered. The message to my team is that they are the eyes and ears as well, and that if they see things that they are worried about, report them.”

Expensive alternative

The alternative to driver monitoring is the saturation of the entire 10,000-mile rail network with remote sensors.

But coupled with measures to shore up thousands of miles of slopes, that could cost billions.

Gareth Evans, head of Rail Technology at Network Rail, said such an approach would be so expensive as to be impractical.

He said: “We have to take a risk-based approach to understanding the earthwork assets because there’s so many of them it would not be affordable for the taxpayer to deploy instruments on every single metre.”

The Aslef train drivers’ union said that, whatever the bill, everything possible must be done to enhance safety and avoid a repeat of Stonehaven.

Mick Whelan, Aslef general secretary, said: “Stonehaven casts a long shadow and we must ensure that such an accident never happens in this country again.

“While we understand that there are many cuttings, embankments, bridges, and viaducts on Britain’s railway network, it is vital that Network Rail has the money to ensure that landslips, and other failures, do not happen.”

In the recent Cumbria incident, an Avanti Pendolino tilting train ran into a mudslide immediately south of a summit near Shap, which is the highest point on the West Coast Main Line in England.

While the train was operating at only 83mph, two-thirds of its top speed, and the landslide was shallow – estimates suggest a depth of just a few feet – the impact was sufficient to lift the wheels of the leading carriage off the rails and into the gap between the tracks.

It then ran for more than half a kilometre before coming to a standstill. Extensive damage was caused to the train and track, though just four of the 95 people on board were hurt.

At the Pendolino’s top speed of 125mph, and with a full load of 600-plus passengers, however, the outcome could have been very different.

The RAIB’s preliminary report said a drainage ditch has been flooded because of the heavy rain, leading the slope of the embankment to become too muddy and causing the landslide.

Yet, worryingly, monitors were in operation in the area but seemingly failed to detect the landslip. A Network Rail spokesman declined to comment further on concerns regarding monitoring equipment.

Sensors failure

Network Rail, which is due to be merged with nationalised train operators to create Great British Railways (GBR), is responsible for more than 190,000 individual “earthworks” – which include embankments and soil and rock cuttings.

Rock cuttings are the most likely to suffer some kind of landslide, but they can be covered up to catch any falling stones.

It is soil cuttings that have become the main focus of monitoring, which over 20 years has progressed from visual inspections through instruments drilled into the ground to lower-cost sensor technologies that directly alert rail control rooms to any ground movement.

Simon Abbott, Network Rail head of geotechnics, said that in cases where “sensors are taken out by a large failure” there are many examples of them “having worked really well”.

However, he said that monitoring is “not a silver bullet”.

“Sending people out is a resource we don’t really have at our disposal any more, so technology is playing more of a part,” he said.

Mr Abbott added: “You could put more investment into any aspect of that overall approach and see value, but it’s how you use an appropriate level of funding across the spectrum.

“We’ve got a huge amount of work that we’ve identified where we can justify intervening, and we’ve also got sites where we want to strengthen how we manage risk.”

Network Rail has also placed greater focus on weather forecasting in the wake of Stonehaven, with trains drastically slowed down in areas experiencing heavy rain.

Following the recent Cumbria incident, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, pledged that safety would remain the number one priority for the industry through nationalisation.

She said: “It is right that Network Rail are making sure they shape the way in which they are spending money to address the changing climate.

“I do want proper monitoring to be in place to make sure that we reduce disruption to a minimum and that we can keep our railways operating as safely as possible.

“I don’t ever want to be in a position again where I’m doing a broadcast round and find out that there has been a train derailment.”

However, Richard Holden, the Conservative transport spokesman, said he is concerned that Labour’s creation of GBR will erect barriers to the work needed to combat landslides.

He said: “We want to see more and better monitoring, but you get that through innovation. And to get the Treasury to invest in innovation you’ve got to have a growing rail sector.

“You need a railway which is operating busily so that the risk of not doing something can be calculated in financial terms. If stopping a popular service running is going to involve a huge amount of cost, it’s going to get national attention, as the Shap incident did.

“But as the railway is brought under state control, who is going to be fighting for resources at a time when the Government is being squeezed on multiple different fronts?”

data.title: Where’s Dave? South East Water boss must come out of hiding
data.description:
It takes a special kind of ineptitude to challenge the stinking mess that is Thames Water for the accolade of Britain’s worst-run water supplier.

After all, Thames is genuinely considering approving a £2.1m retention scheme for senior managers in the coming days, in what would surely rank as the most shocking example of rewarding failure that has been seen in a long time.

If the bonus award goes ahead then Ian Pearson, the chairman of the remuneration committee, should be forced to shovel sewage every weekend for the rest of his working life. Even the cold-blooded vulture funds that now control Thames’s fate are said to be appalled that the payout could go ahead, which tells you just how tone deaf the board is.

Yet somehow the nodding dogs at Thames may be upstaged by their bungling counterparts at South East Water, who appear to be doing everything in their power to demonstrate that they’re not fit to flush the toilet, never mind run a company that provides water to 2.3 million people.

South East Water has a terrible track record of letting down its customers but nothing it has done before compares to the current crisis that has engulfed the organisation after thousands of homes in Kent were left without water for days.

What ensures that this latest escapade is destined to be remembered as one of the great corporate omnishambles of our times is the company’s response – or, rather, total lack of it – to what can only be described as almost apocalyptic scenes in some parts of Kent and East Sussex, including the historic spa town of Royal Tunbridge Wells.

It’s bad enough that South East Water customers have been left without clean running water for six whole days.

Can you imagine what it’s like to not be able to flush the toilet, clean your teeth or have a shower for that length of time, or what that does to some critical services?

Doctor’s surgeries and local banks have had to shut, care homes are having to ration water, children are missing out on school and local businesses have lost thousands of pounds worth of trade in the run-up to Christmas.

Some exasperated residents have simply packed up and left, possibly in search of a river to bathe in – that’s if they can find one in Britain that hasn’t been turned into a sea of floating turds.

So what has the boss of South East Water had to say about the bedlam that the company has inflicted on residents – some sage advice, perhaps? Reassurance at least? A profuse apology, surely?

Wrong – absolutely nothing. Not a word. At least, not publicly anyway.

That’s right, a man who was paid £456,000 last year has yet to show his face during this debacle. It’s tempting to think he’s too busy working day and night in a hard hat, high-vis and wellies to solve this mess – but don’t hold your breath.

Perhaps it’s the shame that has prevented him from showing his face. After all, whatever Dreadful Dave Hinton does between now and the conclusion of his career – that is of course if this doesn’t bring it to an end prematurely – will surely never be enough to compensate for inflicting the unthinkable on an area renowned for its natural hydration.

For nearly 400 years, people have been travelling to Royal Tunbridge Wells to “take the waters” – a term first coined by Dudley, Third Baron North, after discovering the restorative mineral rich properties of the town’s Chalybeate Spring at the turn of the 17th century.

Imagine what the nobleman would make of the town today, transformed into a veritable parched desert under the calamitous stewardship of Hinton and colleagues. The sorry sight of the town’s water-starved inhabitants reduced to using temporary portable loos erected in a local car park and begging for bottled water will have him turning in his grave.

If that wasn’t enough to persuade Hinton to come out of hiding, then how about the revelation that the water isn’t safe to drink after supplies were restored to some homes on Wednesday?

Wrong again. Instead, the company sent out poor Neil Hudson, South East Water’s head of water quality – an oxymoron if ever there was one – to be grilled by a dogged local BBC news reporter after the company advised residents to boil their water before using it.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” the journalist asked, entirely reasonably.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impacts whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson stammered.

“But some won’t get that message will they?” the reporter pointed out. Hudson needn’t have replied – his increasingly flustered demeanour was worth a thousand words.

If that wasn’t confirmation that this is an organisation wholly unsuited to providing critical household services, then the news that the water works at the centre of the supply crisis was judged to be a significant risk to public health a year ago is about as damning as it gets. Ditto the bombshell that it’ll be at least another ten days before supplies are back to normal.

The assessment of Mike Martin, Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, that the situation was now “an emergency and the Secretary of State needs to deploy national resources to help on the ground” is a perfectly reasonable one.

It’s not as if this is an isolated incident. Customers have suffered supply outages before and in 2023, Ofwat said South East Water had the record for interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Perhaps the reason why Hinton is still in post is that nobody can find him to give him his marching orders.

Still, that hasn’t stopped the local newspaper from trying to track him down.

“Where’s Dave?” KentOnline asked on Wednesday, to which one reader responded: “Wherever he is, I bet he has enough water!”

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: The sad fate of the Catholic Herald is a warning of the dangers of US investment firms
data.description:
The US investment firm Redbird has withdrawn from its deal to buy The Telegraph. The fate of another iconic British publication, the Catholic Herald, which was bought in 2023 by $3.4bn alternative asset firm GEM NY, offers a cautionary tale about the dangers of being taken over by private equity firms, especially American ones.

After only two years of GEM’s involvement, the Catholic Herald is about to go to only four issues a year, down from 12, and most of its staff have walked out.

The once mighty Herald, which in its post-war glory days used to boast a 100,000 weekly Sunday newspaper circulation and operate from its own building off Fleet Street, now has its British HQ in a “hot-desk” corner of Westminster Library. When I joined the magazine part-time in 2019, it was one of the most civilised places in Britain, or possibly the world, to work. We operated from Herald House, a Dickensian old building next to the Barbican – with its own church – owned by the diocese of Westminster.

That it was falling to pieces and you had to walk up five floors to reach editorial didn’t matter. The staff, then headed by Luke Coppen, were courteous, brilliant, marvellously eccentric and, most importantly, superb at what they did, miraculously putting out 50 extremely high-quality weekly issues a year.

I realised at the time what a privilege it was for a young journalist like me to be working in such a professional and historic media organisation.

Lack of money, however, became a serious problem during the pandemic when churches were closed and distribution effectively cut off. The directors were under enormous pressure to find new investment. Finally, the New York-based GEM NY, who had recently had success with selling an iconic French fashion magazine for $57m, acquired a controlling stake in the 135-year old magazine which had a 100-year-plus digital archive and whose writers used to include GK Chesterton, Evelyn Waugh and Graham Greene.

GEM NY was run by “good New York Catholics with a lot of money who wanted to give something back”, we were told. They would hire brilliant consultants who would turn the Catholic Herald into a global brand and we would just continue as normal. With America having 80 million Catholics, compared to just 3 million in the UK, having a new focus on the USA – where conservative Catholics help to shape political conversation in a way that doesn’t happen here – made sense.

At first, then-chairman and editor William Cash, and later his successor Serenhedd James, retained editorial control, but bit by bit, the control waned. Various different editors – there have been three in the last two years – were told what to insert into their editor’s letters. Senior-level meetings, which took place weekly over Zoom, quickly evidenced the stark difference between the British and American mentality. When James asked for staff to be given contracts, he was told: “If people want job security, they can go and work somewhere else”.

Penny-pinching became an obsession. In one meeting, bizarrely, James was criticised by the board’s representatives, with raised voices, for accepting an invitation to have coffee at Archbishop’s House with Cardinal Vincent Nichols, the leader of the Catholic Church in England and Wales, and for attending the Tablet’s annual Advent party at the Athenaeum. An impression of profligacy was suggested, despite the fact that neither event cost the Catholic Herald a penny.

The idea that a magazine editor might occasionally do some networking seemed alien to GEM, whose main priority, it was becoming increasingly clear, was trying to squeeze the Herald into commercial profit when, for over a century, its financial supporters – such as Sir Rocco Forte and Lord (Conrad) Black – invested because they believed in the Herald as an important cause, and the traditional Catholic values it stood up for.

That GEM were not the benevolent owners the staff had hoped quickly became apparent. Under the banner “digital first” – Americans, like the communists, are very keen on slogans – cost-cutting fast became the primary objective and if that meant that loyal staff of many years were to lose their jobs, so be it. “Benedetta’s termination”, a botched attempt at sacking a hard-working young Italian woman who had worked at the magazine for over 10 years, for bringing in less money than she earned, became a by-word for GEM’s chaos and ruthlessness.

James, who had not been consulted about the move before the meeting in which it was raised, fought back hard. He had no trouble proving that the member of staff made more money for the magazine that she earned, and saved her job – at least for the time being. All might have remained calm had not the AI-generated minutes of that meeting then been sent to the wrong mailing list by GEM’s systems – including to the particular member of staff herself.

Meanwhile, the demoralising attacks on editorial staff started slowly but quickly ramped up when GEM took on a digital-communications agent, based in Malta, in an effort to bring in paid subscriptions and market the brand. We were told they would build a new website and bring in structural changes which would improve the Herald’s exposure online, which all sounded sensible.

Editorial control was effectively handed over to this new digital agency. When we were told categorically by the agency’s CEO, that we, the editors, would no longer be allowed to write headlines or choose photographs for online articles, our news editor – a veteran journalist of over 30 years experience, including at national newspapers – walked out. It later became clear that the magazine’s editor would no longer have the final say over what appeared on the cover of the magazine; that too would belong to the agency’s CEO. For James it was the last straw; he resigned at the start of September. The Herald’s highly-talented production manager has also gone.

While this was all happening, I was on bereavement leave from my job as online editor following the death of my husband from cancer. Not long after my husband’s funeral I decided I was ready to come back to work but was told initially that I was not allowed to and that the board wanted me to take a further month off.

Slightly perplexed, I asked colleagues what they thought the reasoning was, only to be told that GEM had implemented a major reshuffle in my absence, and that my job had been given to a junior member of staff with little journalism experience – and that the owners were now not sure what to do. It seemed they had taken the gamble, based on the fact that I was now a widowed mother of 3 children under seven, that I wouldn’t want to or be able to come back to work.

They then panic-offered me the job of deputy editor, but for two further months were unable to tell me what that job would involve and how much I would be paid to do it. In the meantime, under my junior colleague, AI prompts started appearing in the Herald’s online copy, prompting readers to complain to me and other members of the team. I subsequently discovered that this colleague was being paid £1000 more a month than I was offered for my so-called “promotion”. Unsurprisingly, I have also now left the Catholic Herald. Three months on, I still have not received any acknowledgement of my resignation from the board.

On November 24, after nearly two months with no permanent editor, GEM consultants announced that after, in their own words, a “comprehensive international search” they had appointed as editor of the Catholic Herald the CEO of the Malta-based communications firm brought into to work on brand strategy – a digital marketing executive with no experience of journalism and limited experience of publishing.

I highlight only a few examples of the chaos that has ensued since GEM NY took over the Catholic Herald. Another sticking point which has clear parallels with the doomed Telegraph Redbird deal is inflated valuation. When they attempted to raise new funding via a “crowd-funding” round last year, GEM valued the Herald at an eye-watering £10m, almost six times more than they had originally reportedly paid, despite the Herald losing money and increasing its debt.

The “digital first” strategy has resulted in more website traffic – more clicks – but actual paid subscriptions remain challenging. Investment is badly needed to engage subscribers. What print subscribers – who have paid for 10-12 issues a year – will think of now receiving just four remains to be seen. One expects some unholy correspondence. The only hope left is that GEM will see sense before it’s too late and sell it for a fair market price.

Such an attempt to buy the magazine was made last year by Catholic convert media entrepreneur George Farmer, who sits on the board of GB News. But, alas, George and his US investor consortium were rebuffed. Above all, the Herald needs to be sold to someone who isn’t so naive as to think that they will become rich by buying it. The best they may hope for is to be fast tracked through the Pearly Gates.

data.title: Jamie Oliver to relaunch collapsed Italian restaurant chain
data.description:
Jamie Oliver is resurrecting his Italian restaurant brand six years after the chain’s collapse.

The celebrity chef’s casual dining venture, Jamie’s Italian, fell into administration in 2019 under the weight of an £80m debt pile following a period of rapid expansion.

At its peak, it had around 40 sites across the UK with an annual turnover of more than £100m. However, diners shunned the chain amid complaints that the quality of the food had dropped.

Mr Oliver said he was “going to take it slowly”, with the relaunch starting with a restaurant on Leicester Square in central London as a joint venture with Prezzo owner Brava Hospitality.

“As a chef, having the chance to return to the high street is incredibly important to me. In theory, it’s not the easiest time to return, but conversely, I think it’s the perfect time,” he said.

“I believe the mid-market needs excitement, surprise and delight, and that’s exactly what I am planning on delivering.”

The first Jamie’s Italian opened in Oxford in 2008, with Mr Oliver at the time saying he intended to “positively disrupt mid-market dining” with higher-quality ingredients.

Following the expansion, its fortunes began to decline rapidly amid complaints that the food quality had slipped.

Jon Knight, then the company’s chief executive, said at the time that the chain had become “complacent” and failed to respond to changes in dining trends.

“As a business, we lost our way,” he told the Casual Dining Show a year before it fell into administration. “We hadn’t acknowledged just how much the high street had changed over the last 10 years.”

At one point, the business was reportedly only hours away from bankruptcy, prompting Mr Oliver to inject nearly £13m of his own money to stave off collapse. Mr Oliver later told the Financial Times the chain had “simply run out of cash”.

By 2019, all but three of the remaining 25 Jamie’s Italian restaurants had closed, resulting in around 1,000 job losses.

The group called in administrators KPMG, blaming a “very difficult trading environment” and failure to secure new investment. The business owed roughly £83.3m to its creditors, including loans, overdrafts and rent.

Several councils were left tens of thousands out of pocket when the chain collapsed, and unpaid business rates were written off. Jamie’s Italian owed £1.4m to local authorities, including £55,000 to Tower Hamlets in east London and £80,000 to neighbouring Newham.

“Mr Oliver will appreciate how important business rates are to fill the gap left by the Government to fund vital public services and tackle food poverty,” Joshua Garfield, a Newham councillor, told MyLondon in 2022. “I hope he’ll see to it personally that these bills are paid.”

A progress report by Interpath administrators showed that Mr Oliver recouped £2.4m from the collapse of the business.

The Leicester Square branch of Jamie’s Italian will open in spring 2026.

James Brown, of Brava Hospitality Group, said: “A lot of time and energy has gone into evolving the Jamie’s Italian concept to make that vision a reality.”

Ed Loftus, of Jamie Oliver Restaurants, added: “Our priority is making the first location exceptional; the public will ultimately determine how quickly and how far we grow.”

The brand still operates more than 30 restaurants in 15 countries.

data.title: HSBC bans Christmas jumpers over fears it could trigger customers
data.description:
HSBC has banned staff from wearing Christmas jumpers over fears they could upset some customers.

The bank has told its employees not to wear the festive items this year after some customers complained about the professionalism of the outfits.

It added that its branches were spaces where customers received support with bereavement and financial difficulty, services that require “professionalism and sensitivity”.

According to The Sun, which first reported the news, there were also concerns that it would add unnecessary complications if staff had to take off a Christmas jumper before a serious conversation.

Last year, employees at one branch were photographed wearing festive knitwear, including one featuring the Grinch in a Santa hat alongside the phrase “Merry whatever”.

It is understood that the decision had nothing to do with cultural sensitivities and HSBC said staff were still encouraged to celebrate Christmas in other ways, such as through decorating local branches, festive lunches or team celebrations.

A spokesman said: “We are encouraging colleagues to celebrate Christmas in branches in lots of different ways, including decorating branches, team celebrations and festive lunches.

“However, it’s important to also remember that branches are spaces where customers receive a range of specialist services that require professionalism and sensitivity. This year, we have asked colleagues in customer-facing roles not to wear Christmas jumpers to reflect this.”

While festive jumpers have been banned at HSBC, the Church of England recently gave its blessing to even the most lurid Christmas sweaters.

Nicholas Papadopulos, the Dean of Salisbury, wrote in a booklet produced by Church House Publishing: “You see, Christmas jumpers are tasteless. And here’s the thing. God is tasteless too. That’s actually the heart of the good news. God is without a shred of cultural or aesthetic judgment. That must be so.”

Mr Papadopulos added that God is also a “knitter” who is thanked for “carefully weaving us into being”, adding: “God has no taste. God does not discriminate. God does not exclude. So wear that jumper with pride.”

In 2023, HSBC shifted to more casual uniforms for its staff. These included a new range of jumpsuits, chinos and jeans, which it said would mirror the “more casual new look of the banks’ branches”.

The bank has 327 branches across the country. Last year, it said visits had fallen by 65pc over the previous five years. Since 2015, HSBC has closed 743 outlets. However, it has committed to announcing no new branch closures until “at least” 2026.

data.title: Plans for Britain’s biggest Muslim cemetery revived by Issa brothers
data.description:
The billionaire Issa brothers have revived plans to build Britain’s biggest Muslim cemetery despite local opposition.

Mohsin and Zuber Issa, best known for their £7bn takeover of Asda, have been trying for the past four years to build the giant graveyard near Oswaldtwistle in Lancashire – a village with a population of 10,815 people.

The Memorial Garden, as it will be known, would surpass the Gardens of Peace in east London as the UK’s largest Muslim cemetery if built, and include a funeral parlour and prayer halls.

It has been twice rejected by local officials. The Issa brothers had withdrawn their plans in October after planning bosses said it would have a “harmful effect on [the] appearance of the area”.

Officers also claimed that the cemetery would pose an “unacceptable risk of pollution of groundwater”.

However, the Issas have now said they remain “committed” to the project after dismissing an application by campaigners to use the site for a nature reserve.

Zak Khan, a local Conservative councillor, and Vinette Davitt, a community activist, said they had written to the Issa Foundation – the brothers’ charity – suggesting they instead convert the land into a “network of enhanced habitats and attractive landscapes”.

However, a spokesman for the foundation said it would instead reapply to build the graveyard.

The charity said it had invited Mr Khan to meet to discuss his proposal, “but this offer was declined due to community concerns about the cemetery project”.

It added: “We remain of the view that our proposal respectfully protects the land from built development while equally offering environmental and community benefits. In the meantime, until a scheme with broad stakeholder support can be progressed, we will continue to farm the land and proactively maintain the site.”

The cemetery was seen as part of a wider move to grow their influence in Lancashire, including building a mosque with 95ft-high minarets and a £3m dental surgery.

The Issa brothers, who made their multibillion-pound fortune through growing petrol forecourt empire EG Group, are also building a series of mega mansions close to where they grew up in the Brookhouse area.

However, the cemetery project has been hit by a series of setbacks, with a local Tory councillor claiming that more than 10,000 residents are in opposition to the scheme.

Last year, they submitted a scaled-down planning application for the project - changing it from 35,000 burial plots to 12,250 and reducing the number of car parking spaces to 387 spaces, from more than 660 previously.

However, the project has continued to face opposition, with campaigners claiming it remains “huge” and takes up “over half of the green belt” in the area.

The Issa Foundation said its advisers had recommended that it continues to work on the cemetery plans, adding: “We have consistently prioritised high-quality landscaping, respecting public access, and celebrating the natural character of Oswaldtwistle and the surrounding area.”

data.title: BP abandons UK green energy plant in blow to Miliband
data.description:
BP has shelved plans to build a major hydrogen project in Teesside in a fresh blow to Ed Miliband’s net zero plans.

The oil giant withdrew its request to the Government on Monday to build the project after it clashed with separate plans backed by Sir Keir Starmer to construct the largest data centre in Europe.

It comes after Downing Street selected a former steel site in Teesside as the home for its second AI Growth Zone, with Labour banking on the technology to help turbocharge the economy.

The two proposed sites were earmarked on overlapping plots of land, pitting the Energy Secretary – who was relying on the BP project to meet his net zero ambitions – against the Prime Minister and Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary.

BP said a decision by the Labour-led Redcar and Cleveland council to throw its weight behind the data centre ultimately made its project unviable.

In a letter to the Energy Department, a spokesman for the BP-backed H2Teesside project insisted its decision had “not been taken lightly”.

It said it still believed hydrogen had “an important role to play in the future energy market”.

A spokesman for BP also blamed “material changes in circumstances” including “a planning application being granted locally for a data centre on the same piece of land” for its decision to withdraw from the project.

Sir Keir has claimed that AI will help the Government deliver a “decade of national renewal”, with plans for the new AI growth zone now expected to be announced before Christmas.

Tensions between the Energy Department and Mr Kyle over the competing projects escalated to such an extent that the Business Secretary even explored legal routes to attempt to influence Mr Miliband’s decision.

Mr Miliband had already twice delayed a decision on whether to grant the development consent order (DCO) to start producing “blue” hydrogen from natural gas, and then capture and store the carbon emissions.

A decision was due on Thursday Dec 4, but it is understood that BP has withdrawn its application for the DCO ahead of an announcement in order to make way for the data centre to be built.

The H2Teesside scheme was announced by BP in 2021 and had been slated to deliver more than 10pc of a plan for a clean power system by 2030.

However, The Telegraph revealed in May that BP was considering scaling back or even cancelling the 1.2-gigawatt project, which was expected to generate enough energy to power a million homes.

Several sources suggested that BP had been reluctant to back down from the project amid concerns it was struggling to secure sufficient interest from customers to make the investment worthwhile.

Chemicals giant Sabic, which was separately expected to partner with BP, announced the closure of its site in the North East in June.

A spokesman for BP said: “Due to material changes in circumstances on the Teesworks site, including a planning application being granted locally for a data centre on the same piece of land, we have taken the decision not to progress the development of H2Teesside and have withdrawn our Development Consent Order application for the project.

“We continue to move forward with other projects on Teesside, including our investments in Net Zero Teesside Power and the Northern Endurance Partnership, and remain an active partner in the region.”

A Government spokesman said: “This is a decision taken by BP.

“We continue to provide a route for hydrogen projects in Teesside, including Tees Green Hydrogen, which is moving towards final investment decision, along with several other projects creating high-quality jobs for the region.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The Shard’s brickmaker collapses into administration
data.description:
The brickmaker that supplied The Shard has collapsed into administration amid a protracted slowdown in construction.

York Handmade Brick filed a notice of intention to appoint administrators on Friday, according to court records, and is represented by Addleshaw Goddard.

The manufacturer supplied 90,000 bespoke bricks to The Shard, which opened in 2013. The 87-storey skyscraper is predominantly glass and steel but has bricks on its first few floors, including a dramatic brickwork façade.

York bricks have also been used at London Bridge railway station, with 100,000 provided for the station’s overhaul ahead of its reopening in 2017.

Other projects it has been involved in include Magdalene College Library in Cambridge, which won the Royal Institute of British Architects’s Stirling Prize in 2022.

The company also supplied the bricks for the restoration of St Albans Cathedral in Hertfordshire, the oldest site of continuous Christian worship in Britain, the Westgate Oxford shopping centre redevelopment and York Racecourse.

Based at Alne, near Easingwold, the brickmaker has been running since 1988. It was founded by David Armitage, a fifth-generation brickmaker, and remains a family business. Guy Armitage, David’s son, is now the company’s managing director.

The company’s collapse into administration comes at a torrid time for construction suppliers and builders, as rising costs and a downturn in building heap pressure on the industry.

In a trading update on Tuesday, fellow brick manufacturer Michelmersh warned of a “notable slowdown” in the industry during the final quarter of the year.

Michelmersh said activity has been hit by a “challenging macro-economic outlook and the uncertainty of UK Budget policy announcements, which have adversely impacted both consumer sentiment and investment decision making”.

Builders were left disappointed by last week’s Budget, including Richard Beresford, chief executive of the National Federation of Builders, who said in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s statement that the “industry will remain in the mud”.

“Death by a thousand taxes has already killed off many businesses, with insolvency rates still high within the construction industry,” he said.

The construction sector shrank at its fastest pace in more than five years in October, according to the S&P Global UK Construction PMI index.

Brick makers are also among the industries struggling with high energy costs, and relief for manufacturers has been pushed back to 2027.

York Handmade Brick were contacted for comment, while Addleshaw Goddard declined to comment.

data.title: Heathrow to fine drivers £80 for overly long farewells
data.description:
People who take too long to bid farewell to loved ones at Heathrow face an £80 fine for overstaying their welcome.

A new 10-minute limit for unloading bags and wishing passengers a safe journey at Britain’s busiest airport will leave no time for drawn-out goodbyes.

The cap will be introduced for drivers at Heathrow’s forecourts from January as part of a shake-up that will push drop-off charges up from £6 to £7 in an inflation-busting 17pc increase.

Unlike most other major airports in the UK, Heathrow has not previously sought to limit the time people can spend dropping off friends, family and business colleagues.

However, hubs such as Gatwick and Luton allow over-stayers to pay by the minute rather than hitting them with a hefty fine.

Clive Wratten, the chief executive of the Business Travel Association, a lobby group, said the higher fees and strict time limit risk punishing passengers unnecessarily.

He said: “There’s next to no justification for this. These charges do nothing to improve the passenger experience or reliability of access to Heathrow. All it does is raise costs.”

Heathrow claimed that the 10-minute maximum stay, to be introduced across all of its terminal forecourts, would ease congestion and improve traffic flows.

It said: “This will enhance the customer experience for those using the terminal drop off as it is intended.”

It said 95pc of drop-offs take less than 10 minutes and that the time limit is expected to have a “minimal impact”.

A spokesman added: “Most people just drop off and go, they don’t hang around. It’s very busy, and we need to move people on. There just isn’t space in those terminal forecourts.”

One response to the scheme, however, could be for drivers who need more than 10 minutes to circle round and return to the drop-off area, accruing a £14 fee rather than the £80 fine, potentially adding to congestion.

Heathrow said the £7 basic charge – also backed up by an £80 fine for non-payment, reduced to £40 if settled within 14 days – brings it into line with other airports and is aimed partly at encouraging “options for travel that have a lower environmental impact”.

While Gatwick already charges £7 for the first 10 minutes on its forecourts, people can stay for longer at a cost of £1 per minute, up to a maximum of 30 minutes and £27.

Luton operates a similar system, charging £7 for 10 minutes and £1 per minute thereafter, again with a maximum stay of 30 minutes.

Manchester and Stansted airports have somewhat lower basic charges but a flat £25 rate for people who extend their time in the drop-off areas.

Heathrow said blue-badge holders will be exempt from the maximum stay limit and will still be able to use drop-off zones for free, so long as they register their trip.

data.title: Rightmove under pressure as activist investor takes £250m stake
data.description:
An activist investor has bought a near £250m stake in Rightmove, piling fresh pressure on the embattled property giant.

London-based Independent Franchise Partners (IFP) has built up a 5.8pc stake in the house-buying portal in recent weeks, becoming Rightmove’s third-biggest shareholder.

The investment was made after the company warned its profit growth will slow next year because of investment in AI. That announcement wiped £1bn off Rightmove’s market value.

IFP has in the past led activist campaigns against the likes of Japanese brewer Kirin – unsuccessfully pushing it to spin-off its non-beer assets – and is preparing to push for change at cosmetics company Shiseido.

The investment group is also one of the largest independent shareholders in Rupert Murdoch’s News Corp and Fox Corp. IFP publicly spoke out against plans for the two to recombine in 2022.

Founded in 2009 by a group of former Morgan Stanley employees, IFP has around $22bn (£16bn) in assets under management. It describes its approach as one that focuses on generating “attractive long-term returns” for clients “with a strong capital preservation bias”. IFP declined to comment.

IFP’s link to the Murdoch media empire is likely to reignite speculation that Rightmove could be a takeover target. Last year the property website rejected a £6.2bn takeover approach from REA Group, an Australian online property company owned by Mr Murdoch’s News Corp.

At the time it rejected the bid, Rightmove argued that it “materially” undervalued the business, pointing to “long-term growth and returns”.

But the £4bn company is now reeling from a dramatic slump in share prices, with stocks plunging by just over 30pc in the past six months.

Anthony Codling, an analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said: “If nothing changes with the share price, you could understand why [investors] might think [bids would return].”

Shares in Rightmove rose 1.8pc following The Telegraph’s report.

Rightmove’s grip on Britain’s property market makes it an attractive asset. It is the biggest property listings website in the UK by site traffic and property numbers, garnering more than 80pc of all time spent by consumers on property portals. The FTSE 100 company advertises one million UK homes every month.

However, investors have lately become concerned about the potential threat posed by AI. Chatbots could reinvent the way people hunt for houses, allowing people to ask for tailored listings.

Rightmove sought to counter the threat by unveiling plans to spend £60m on AI last month, including launching automated valuations, new conversational searches and visualisation tools. Johan Svanstrom, its chief executive, said AI was becoming “absolutely central” to its strategy.

But the announcement sent shares in the company tumbling after Rightmove said the investment would take three years to pay off, affecting profits until 2028.

The heavy spending comes against a backdrop of a subdued property market, with buyers and sellers reluctant to commit ahead of last month’s Budget and now facing uncertainty ahead of the introduction of a new council tax surcharge in 2028.

Rightmove also faces the threat of a £1bn lawsuit from estate agents who claim it abused its market dominance to charge “unfair” prices, backed by the litigation funding arm of US activist Elliott. The property portal has said it is “confident in the value we provide to our partners”.

Since August, Rightmove has lost £2.3bn of value. Giles Thorne, of Jefferies, said: “The more any share price goes down, by definition, the higher the probability an activist comes in.

“It’s just the rules of the game because they’ll see grounds for management to take some kind of strategic action that would push the share price up.”

Mr Codling said: “I would argue Rightmove’s management should focus just on running the business to the best of their ability and let the share price take care of itself.”

Rightmove has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Rightmove under pressure as activist investor takes £250m stake
data.description:
An activist investor has bought a near £250m stake in Rightmove, piling fresh pressure on the embattled property giant.

London-based Independent Franchise Partners (IFP) has built up a 5.8pc stake in the house-buying portal in recent weeks, becoming Rightmove’s third-biggest shareholder.

The investment was made after the company warned its profit growth will slow next year because of investment in AI. That announcement wiped £1bn off Rightmove’s market value.

IFP has in the past led activist campaigns against the likes of Japanese brewer Kirin – unsuccessfully pushing it to spin-off its non-beer assets – and is preparing to push for change at cosmetics company Shiseido.

The investment group is also one of the largest independent shareholders in Rupert Murdoch’s News Corp and Fox Corp. IFP publicly spoke out against plans for the two to recombine in 2022.

Founded in 2009 by a group of former Morgan Stanley employees, IFP has around $22bn (£16bn) in assets under management. It describes its approach as one that focuses on generating “attractive long-term returns” for clients “with a strong capital preservation bias”. IFP declined to comment.

IFP’s link to the Murdoch media empire is likely to reignite speculation that Rightmove could be a takeover target. Last year the property website rejected a £6.2bn takeover approach from REA Group, an Australian online property company owned by Mr Murdoch’s News Corp.

At the time it rejected the bid, Rightmove argued that it “materially” undervalued the business, pointing to “long-term growth and returns”.

But the £4bn company is now reeling from a dramatic slump in share prices, with stocks plunging by just over 30pc in the past six months.

Anthony Codling, an analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said: “If nothing changes with the share price, you could understand why [investors] might think [bids would return].”

Shares in Rightmove rose 1.8pc following The Telegraph’s report.

Rightmove’s grip on Britain’s property market makes it an attractive asset. It is the biggest property listings website in the UK by site traffic and property numbers, garnering more than 80pc of all time spent by consumers on property portals. The FTSE 100 company advertises one million UK homes every month.

However, investors have lately become concerned about the potential threat posed by AI. Chatbots could reinvent the way people hunt for houses, allowing people to ask for tailored listings.

Rightmove sought to counter the threat by unveiling plans to spend £60m on AI last month, including launching automated valuations, new conversational searches and visualisation tools. Johan Svanstrom, its chief executive, said AI was becoming “absolutely central” to its strategy.

But the announcement sent shares in the company tumbling after Rightmove said the investment would take three years to pay off, affecting profits until 2028.

The heavy spending comes against a backdrop of a subdued property market, with buyers and sellers reluctant to commit ahead of last month’s Budget and now facing uncertainty ahead of the introduction of a new council tax surcharge in 2028.

Rightmove also faces the threat of a £1bn lawsuit from estate agents who claim it abused its market dominance to charge “unfair” prices, backed by the litigation funding arm of US activist Elliott. The property portal has said it is “confident in the value we provide to our partners”.

Since August, Rightmove has lost £2.3bn of value. Giles Thorne, of Jefferies, said: “The more any share price goes down, by definition, the higher the probability an activist comes in.

“It’s just the rules of the game because they’ll see grounds for management to take some kind of strategic action that would push the share price up.”

Mr Codling said: “I would argue Rightmove’s management should focus just on running the business to the best of their ability and let the share price take care of itself.”

Rightmove has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Trustpilot accused of running ‘mafia-style extortion racket’
data.description:
Trustpilot has been accused of waging “mafia-style extortion campaigns” against businesses to try and force them to sign up to its service.

Shares in the online customer review platform slumped to a two-year low on Thursday after an American research firm accused it of using “illegal tactics” to push companies into signing up for accounts.

Trustpilot disputed the claims, calling them “selective, misleading and framed to support a predetermined narrative”.

Grizzly Research alleges that the Danish company creates accounts for businesses that are not customers and then only lets them manage and respond to negative reviews if they pay for a subscription.

“We believe Trustpilot has created a platform to attract bad reviews and customers must pay to dilute the bad reviews. This created a review system that only seems to express who pays Trustpilot,” Grizzly Research said in a 26-page report.

The US-headquartered firm claimed the strategy was akin to “mafia-style extortion campaigns against non-paying businesses”.

Grizzly Research alleged its findings were based on interviews with Trustpilot customers, those who didn’t subscribe, former employees and third-party industry experts.

It wrote: “We and many experts we consulted believe Trustpilot is ... actively using illegal tactics to manipulate review scores.”

In a statement issued hours after report was published, Trustpilot said the report “omits key context and publicly available facts, creating a false impression and exhibits a lack of understanding of how Trustpilot works. Trust is our guiding principle and is central to everything we do.”

The rebuttal was not enough to stop Trustpilot’s stock price plunging by 32pc on Thursday, its steepest ever one-day decline. The fall, which knocked more than £200m from its market value, brought losses since the start of the year up to almost 60pc.

More than 1.4 million businesses use Trustpilot globally, including FTSE giants Sainsbury’s, Barclays and Next. The company was founded in 2007 and has become a digital Kitemark, allowing customers to review and measure the customer service of a business.

Many companies publicly display their Trustpilot ratings on their websites to reassure customers. More than 200,000 new reviews are posted every day.

Grizzly Research alleged that paid profiles typically see their review score increase from two out of five stars to four out of five, calling this model the “Trustpilot Mafia”. It claimed the company’s “growth relies on extorting businesses while eroding trust in the brand”.

New York-based Grizzly Research was founded five years ago by Siegfried Eggert and has previously targeted companies including SharkNinja, HelloFresh and The Hershey Company.

A spokesman for Grizzly Research confirmed that the company had a short position in Trustpilot shares.

Short-sellers typically make money by betting against a business’s shares in the hope that they fall. The tactic of publishing damaging accusation against a company to prompt a share price is not unusual but is controversial.

Trustpilot floated on the London Stock Exchange at the end of the first quarter in 2021, gaining a valuation of £1.1bn at the time.

The Boston-based hedge fund Sunley House Capital Master is the company’s biggest shareholder, with a stake of 5.4pc. Capital Group, Vanguard and BlackRock also have considerable stakes in Trustpilot.

data.title: Heathrow boss buys ‘extra-loud phone’ after sleeping through airport blackout
data.description:
The boss of Heathrow bought himself an “extra-loud phone” after sleeping through multiple calls when a power outage led to the closure of the airport.

Thomas Woldbye was asleep when his senior staff decided to suspend all flights because of a fire at Hayes electrical substation, which supplies about a third of Heathrow’s electricity needs.

The blaze started at Europe’s busiest airport late on the evening of March 20 and flights were suspended until about 6pm the next day.

A Heathrow-commissioned inquiry led by Ruth Kelly, the former transport secretary, found Mr Woldbye was not woken by alerts sent to his mobile, which was on a bedside table, because it had gone into silent mode “without him being aware”.

Asked at the Airlines 2025 conference in Westminster on Monday if he had since obtained an “extra-loud phone”, Mr Woldbye replied: “Oh, absolutely. And more.”

‘Nothing should depend on me alone’

He said he was “personally devastated” by his phone being on silent, describing it as a “technical glitch”.

But he added that “nothing in this world should be depending on me alone” and “all the right people were there to take the right decisions”.

More than 270,000 passengers’ journeys were disrupted by the incident.

Mr Woldbye, who was paid £3.2m last year, said businesses “need to be able to trust our utilities” and we are “not in a country where everybody needs a back-up”.

He added: “The Neso [National Energy System Operator] report made it very, very clear there were major failures on the part of National Grid and they should have been fixed.

“We should not have been in that situation in the first place.”

The Heathrow boss was also asked whether a similar power outage today would close the airport down again.

He replied: “It would, probably, for about six hours.”

Mr Woldbye added that “lots of procedures” had changed since March to ensure that recovering from another sudden power loss would be “faster”.

One plan included installing “more backup generators”, following criticisms that the airport had replaced diesel-fired equipment with a net zero-compliant biomass plant, which needs mains electricity for it to be started up.

data.title: Channel Tunnel owner blames tax rises as it scraps UK investment
data.description:
The owner of the Channel Tunnel has scrapped all UK investment following the Government’s decision to burden it with tens of millions of pounds in extra taxes.

Eurotunnel said plans to increase its business rates bill by almost 200pc were “unjustified and confiscatory in nature”.

As a result, it said it was suspending plans to develop a freight terminal in Barking, east London, which would have allowed it to run dedicated freight services from France and free up capacity on passenger routes.

The Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which provides the valuations across England and Wales that underpin property taxes, has informed the company that it plans to almost triple the rateable value of its properties from next year.

This would increase Eurotunnel’s annual business rates bill from £22m to £65m in the coming years, although the increase would be phased in, meaning its bill in 2026 will initially jump to £35m.

The VOA has been tasked with overhauling business rates by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, as she wants companies with bigger premises to pay more in levies to help reduce costs for smaller sites.

“Eurotunnel expresses its deep disagreement with this planned increase and considers it is unjustified and confiscatory in nature, and that it threatens Eurotunnel’s investment capacity in Great Britain,” the company said on Thursday.

“Eurotunnel will continue its efforts to negotiate with the VOA and its supervisory authorities until the final valuation decision, expected at the end of March 2026.”

Yann Leriche, the chief executive, told the Financial Times that the move “makes all of our investments loss-making, so we won’t be making any more investments”.

He added: “As of today, we have frozen our investment in rail assets in the UK.”

He said the suspended investment amounted to £15m, while Eurotunnel suggested it could challenge the decision.

Cancelling plans to boost freight through the Channel Tunnel would conflict with the Government’s hopes of taking cargo off the roads by boosting rail.

“Eurotunnel believes that it is currently unduly penalised compared to its competitors whose activities are more carbon-intensive, less-taxed, and some of which are deviating from the social models applicable in France and Great Britain,” the company said.

Earlier this week, the boss of airline Jet2 complained that the Government was treating the travel industry like a “cash cow”.

This month, electric vehicle charging companies wrote to the Treasury warning that plans to introduce business rates on chargers’ parking bays would hurt the adoption of EVs.

A VOA spokesman said: “The VOA is responsible for non-domestic valuations which reflect changes in the property market. All valuations are carried out by experienced professionals in accordance with industry best practice and legal requirements. The VOA does not determine business rates and next year’s liability has not yet been confirmed.

“We have engaged with Getlink and their advisers on multiple occasions over the past 18 months to discuss their valuation and fully explain our approach. These discussions remain ongoing and we are committed to continuing constructive engagement.

“Ratepayers have the right to formally challenge their valuation with the VOA if they believe it is inaccurate. Should they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may appeal to the independent Valuation Tribunal.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Desperate Macron is now ready to use the French media to stifle dissent
data.description:
Emmanuel Macron has long been fixated by fake news and “disinformation”. In a speech in January 2018, he promised to “develop our legal means of protecting democracy against fake news”, singling out the threat from Russia. This obsession, however, is now being used to punish his enemies and marginalise media he dismisses as “far Right”.

This is why, on Monday, Macron’s office denied in a social media post that the president was planning an Orwellian “ministry of truth”. It also rubbished the notion that he had a soft spot for Pravda – the official newspaper of the Soviet Union.

The president was forced on the defensive after a barrage of justified criticism over the weekend from the conservative press and political class. They were alarmed at a speech made by Macron last week in which he declared his wish to introduce a “professional certification” of the media in order to distinguish between what is fact and what is fake.

Macron has stressed that he would not issue the certification of veracity but it would be done by the journalists themselves. This would include greater powers to take broadcasters off air.

In February this year one of France’s most popular free-to-air stations, C8, was shut down by the regulator; the decision had nothing to do with Russian interference but because its editorial control allegedly didn’t respect individual rights.

Many on the Right, including Marine Le Pen, suspected the real reason was because C8 was more populist than progressive. It was, she claimed, a “worrying decision that rules in favour of the Ayatollahs of group-think”.

C8 was part of the media group owned by Vincent Bolloré, the conservative tycoon who is the bête noire of the French elite. His other media interests include a Sunday newspaper, a radio station called Europe 1 and CNews, a television station that has in a short space of time become the most popular source of news in France.

Its success enrages progressives. They refuse to appear on CNews because they claim it is “far Right”. By “far Right”, they mean it doesn’t shy away from confronting troubling questions about mass immigration and insecurity. On the other hand France Televisions, the state broadcaster, prefers not to tackle such sensitive issues.

France’s state-owned radio stations are also tainted by Left-wing bias. A conservative think-tank used artificial intelligence throughout October to monitor the ideological orientation during the morning output of two stations, France Inter and France Culture. France Inter was 60 per cent Left-wing and 16 per cent Right-wing, and France Culture was 66 to six in favour of the Left.

This came as little surprise. In 2023 the head of France Inter, Adèle Van Reeth, said she was proud that the station was “progressive”.

Did she consult the French taxpayer about this ideological orientation? Evidently not, which is perhaps one reason why France Inter has lost 460,000 listeners this year. Public television is also shedding viewers as the French flock instead to independent broadcasters, particularly those which lean to the Right. They don’t believe they spread false information; they believe they tell inconvenient truths that the elite would rather not hear.

CNews doesn’t champion Marine Le Pen but nor does it knock her. It gives her a platform, as does her protégé, Jordan Bardella, increasingly tipped to replace Le Pen as the National Rally’s presidential candidate in 2027.

Bardella said that Macron’s idea to tighten media control was “tampering with freedom of expression”. It was, added Bardella, the act of a president who “has lost power and seeks to maintain it by controlling information”. That’s a truth unlikely to be heard in Macron’s new ministry.
